For **min**: optimal when all $\leq 0$. - **Entering variable** = column with most negative $Z_j - C_j$ (max) or most positive (min). - **Leaving variable** = row with **minimum non-negative ratio** $\theta = X_B / a_{ip}$ (computed only for $a_{ip} > 0$). - **Pivot element** is at the intersection. Apply **Gauss-Jordan** to make it 1 and zero out its column. - **Unbounded LPP:** entering column has all $a_{ip} \leq 0$ — no valid pivot row. - **Infeasible LPP:** artificial variable remains $> 0$ in the final basis (Big-M / Two-Phase). - **Multiple optima:** optimal tableau has $Z_j - C_j = 0$ for a non-basic variable. - **Degeneracy:** $X_B = 0$ for some basic variable — risk of cycling; apply Bland's rule. - Always **verify** the optimal solution by substituting into the **original** constraints. - The **objective value** in the final tableau is $Z^* = C_B^\top X_B$. - For KTU 14-mark questions, expect **at least 3 tableau iterations** — practice mental arithmetic. - **Initial basis** for pure $\leq$ constraints: the slack variables themselves. - **Big-M Method** penalizes artificial variables with a large coefficient $M$ in the objective to force them out of the basis.

topics.title: Big-M Method and Decision Making: Decision Under Certainty
Risk and Uncertainty: Decision Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET612 - QUANTITATIVE TECHNIQUES FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Risk and Uncertainty: Decision Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Decision Tree** is a **graphical-analytical model** used in decision analysis under conditions of **risk and uncertainty**. It represents the sequential structure of a decision problem by mapping out alternative courses of action, chance events, probabilistic outcomes, and their associated payoffs (monetary gains or losses). The tree is analysed using the **roll-back (fold-back) procedure**, computing the **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** at each chance node, working from right (outcomes) to left (initial decision).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a decision tree is a **deterministic search heuristic** for *normative* decision-making that explicitly incorporates:
- **Decision alternatives** (controlled by the decision-maker).
- **States of nature** (uncontrollable future events).
- **Probabilities** (objective or subjective) of each state.
- **Payoffs / Losses** for each combination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Decision Trees fall under *Module 1 – Risk and Uncertainty*. They are the bridge between *probability theory* and *managerial / engineering decision analysis*, and are almost always paired with the concepts of **EMV, Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI), and Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL).**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Choosing Where to Eat"**
>
> Imagine you are deciding **where to eat dinner** before a final exam tomorrow.
>
> - **Decision node (square):** You choose between "Cook at home," "Order from a familiar restaurant," or "Try a new restaurant."
> - **Chance node (circle):** If you try a *new* restaurant, the food may turn out **Great** (probability 0.7) or **Bad** (probability 0.3).
> - **Payoff (₹):** Cook at home = satisfaction **8/10**, Familiar restaurant = **7/10**, New restaurant if Great = **9/10**, New restaurant if Bad = **3/10**.
>
> A decision tree is exactly this *menu of choices with uncertain outcomes*, drawn as branches and sub-branches, with probabilities written beside the chance branches and payoffs at the tips. You then "roll back" from the tips to the root to find the best expected choice.

In engineering contexts, the same logic applies to:
- Choosing between **design alternatives** (steel vs. RCC bridge).
- **Make-or-buy** decisions in manufacturing.
- **Capacity expansion** strategies (build / wait / abandon).
- **R&D project** go / no-go decisions.

## 1.3 Why Decision Trees? (Engineering Utility)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A decision tree forces the decision-maker to:
> 1. **List every feasible alternative** explicitly.
> 2. **Identify all relevant uncertain events** beyond their control.
> 3. **Assign probabilities** (quantifying risk rather than ignoring it).
> 4. **Quantify outcomes** in a common unit (typically ₹ or utility).
> 5. **Compare choices logically** rather than emotionally.

In **operations research, supply chain, finance, civil engineering (tendering), and project management**, decision trees are an essential tool taught in the KTU *Quantitative Techniques for Engineers* syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Decision Tree

A decision tree consists of four primary graphical elements:

| Symbol | Element | Description |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **■ (Square)** | **Decision Node** | A point where the **decision-maker** chooses between two or more alternatives. The branch selected is under the decision-maker's control. |
| **○ (Circle)** | **Chance Node** | A point where an **uncontrollable event** occurs (a state of nature). Multiple probabilistic branches emanate from it. |
| **Branch** | **Arc / Edge** | Represents either an *action* (from decision node) or an *outcome* (from chance node). |
| **▲ (Triangle)** | **End / Payoff Node** | The terminal point of a branch path, showing the **monetary payoff (profit) or loss** for that complete sequence. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention (per KTU / Clemen / Render):** Probabilities appear in **parentheses beside chance branches**, and payoffs appear in **triangles at the tips of the tree**.

## 2.2 The Roll-Back (Fold-Back) Procedure — Step-by-Step Logic

The analysis of a decision tree proceeds **right-to-left** as follows:

1. **Step 1 – Draw the tree** from left (root decision) to right (terminal payoffs). Insert all decision nodes, chance nodes, and their branches.
2. **Step 2 – Assign probabilities** to every chance branch. They must sum to 1.0 at each chance node.
3. **Step 3 – Insert payoffs** at the terminal nodes (triangles). Use a common unit (₹, utility, etc.).
4. **Step 4 – Compute Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** at every chance node:

   $$EMV_{node} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot X_i$$

   where $p_i$ is the probability of outcome $i$ and $X_i$ is the corresponding payoff.

5. **Step 5 – At each decision node**, select the alternative with the **highest EMV** (or the lowest Expected Loss). Prune (cross out with double slash) all other branches.
6. **Step 6 – Continue folding back** until the **root decision node** is reached. The optimal decision is the surviving branch from the root.

## 2.3 Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)

EVPI quantifies the **maximum amount a rational decision-maker should pay** for *perfect* (100 % accurate) information about an uncertain event.

$$EVPI = EVwPI - EMV^{*}$$

where:
- $EVwPI$ = **Expected Value with Perfect Information** = $\sum_{j} p_j \cdot (\text{Best payoff in state } j)$
- $EMV^{*}$ = **Best EMV without information** (i.e., the optimal expected value found by rolling back the tree).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EVPI is an UPPER BOUND on the value of any sample information** (e.g., a market survey, a soil test, a pilot run). If a market study costs more than the EVPI, do **not** buy the information.

## 2.4 Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL) / Regret Table

| Concept | Formula |
|:---|:---|
| **Opportunity Loss (Regret)** in state $S_j$ and decision $d_i$ | $L(d_i, S_j) = \max_k X(d_k, S_j) - X(d_i, S_j)$ |
| **Expected Opportunity Loss of decision $d_i$** | $EOL(d_i) = \sum_j p_j \cdot L(d_i, S_j)$ |
| **Minimum EOL** | $\min_i EOL(d_i)$ |

A fundamental identity of decision theory (which examiners love to test):

$$EMV^{*} + \min EOL = EVwPI$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | $EMV = \sum p_i X_i$ | Expected value of a chance node |
| 2 | $EVwPI = \sum p_j \cdot X^{*}_j$ | Expected value *if* the state of nature were known with certainty |
| 3 | $EVPI = EVwPI - EMV^{*}$ | Max price worth paying for perfect information |
| 4 | $EOL(d_i) = \sum_j p_j \cdot L_{ij}$ | Expected regret of choosing $d_i$ |
| 5 | $EMV^{*} + \min(EOL) = EVwPI$ | Fundamental identity — a 14-mark KTU favourite |
| 6 | $\sum p_i = 1$ | Probability constraint at every chance node |
| 7 | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{EMV}$ | Coefficient of Variation (used to compare risk between alternatives) |
| 8 | $Utility = f(\text{Money})$ | Used when payoffs span very large ranges (e.g., lakhs of ₹) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** In *civil tendering*, EVPI tells the firm the *maximum* it should spend on a geotechnical survey. In *manufacturing*, it bounds the value of a *pilot production run*. In *software engineering project selection*, it quantifies the *value of additional market research*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU-Style Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (OEMET612 typical pattern):**
>
> A company is evaluating two investment alternatives for a new product:
>
> - **Alternative A:** Build a new manufacturing plant.
> - **Alternative B:** Expand the existing plant.
>
> Market demand may turn out **Favourable** or **Unfavourable**. The decision analyst has estimated the following probabilities and payoffs (in ₹ lakhs):
>
> | State of Nature | Probability | Payoff under A | Payoff under B |
> |:---|:---:|---:|---:|
> | Favourable demand | 0.6 | **+10** | **+5** |
> | Unfavourable demand | 0.4 | **−3** | **+2** |
>
> Required:
> (i) Construct the decision tree and determine the optimal decision using EMV.
> (ii) Calculate the Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI).

### 3.1.1 Step 1 – Draw the Decision Tree (logical layout)

The tree has:
- 1 **decision node** (root) → two actions: **A** and **B**.
- 2 **chance nodes** (one under each action) → two outcomes each: Favourable / Unfavourable.
- 4 **terminal payoffs**.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 – Compute EMV at Each Chance Node

**Expected Monetary Value of Alternative A:**

$$EMV_A = (0.6)(\text{+10}) + (0.4)(\text{−3})$$

$$EMV_A = (0.6 \times 10) + (0.4 \times -3)$$

$$EMV_A = 6.00 - 1.20$$

$$EMV_A = 4.80 \; \text{₹ lakhs}$$

**Expected Monetary Value of Alternative B:**

$$EMV_B = (0.6)(\text{+5}) + (0.4)(\text{+2})$$

$$EMV_B = (0.6 \times 5) + (0.4 \times 2)$$

$$EMV_B = 3.00 + 0.80$$

$$EMV_B = 3.80 \; \text{₹ lakhs}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 – Choose the Best Alternative at the Decision Node

$$\max(EMV_A, EMV_B) = \max(4.80, 3.80) = 4.80$$

$$\therefore EMV^{*} = 4.80 \; \text{₹ lakhs}, \quad \text{Optimal Decision: Alternative A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although Alternative B guarantees a *positive* outcome in both states, its lower upside makes its expected value inferior. The decision-maker is **risk-neutral** in pure EMV analysis; the tree favours the higher-EMV action.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 – Expected Value with Perfect Information (EVwPI)

If we *knew* the state of nature in advance, the optimal choice per state would be:

- **If Favourable (p = 0.6):** choose the larger of $\{+10, +5\}$ → **+10** (Choose A).
- **If Unfavourable (p = 0.4):** choose the larger of $\{-3, +2\}$ → **+2** (Choose B).

Hence:

$$EVwPI = (0.6)(\text{+10}) + (0.4)(\text{+2})$$

$$EVwPI = 6.00 + 0.80$$

$$EVwPI = 6.80 \; \text{₹ lakhs}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 – Compute the EVPI

$$EVPI = EVwPI - EMV^{*}$$

$$EVPI = 6.80 - 4.80$$

$$\boxed{EVPI = 2.00 \; \text{₹ lakhs}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The company should not pay more than **₹ 2.00 lakhs** for a *perfect* market survey. If a real survey offers only imperfect information, its price must be even less (the sample information value is bounded above by EVPI).

## 3.2 Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL) Cross-Verification

The opportunity-loss (regret) table is constructed by subtracting each payoff from the **column maximum**.

| State of Nature | Payoff A | Payoff B | Column Max | Loss A | Loss B |
|:---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Favourable | 10 | 5 | 10 | 10 − 10 = 0 | 10 − 5 = 5 |
| Unfavourable | −3 | 2 | 2 | 2 − (−3) = 5 | 2 − 2 = 0 |

**Expected Opportunity Losses:**

$$EOL_A = (0.6)(0) + (0.4)(5) = 0 + 2.00 = 2.00$$

$$EOL_B = (0.6)(5) + (0.4)(0) = 3.00 + 0 = 3.00$$

$$\min(EOL) = 2.00 \;\; \text{(Alternative A — confirms the EMV result)}$$

**Verifying the Fundamental Identity:**

$$EMV^{*} + \min(EOL) = 4.80 + 2.00 = 6.80 = EVwPI \;\; \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The above identity $EMV^{*} + \min(EOL) = EVwPI$ is a **high-weightage** result. Always include it as a verification step in Part B answers.

## 3.3 Decision Tree with Sample (Imperfect) Information — Sequential Roll-Back

A *more advanced* KTU question type involves a **two-stage tree** where the decision-maker may first buy sample information (e.g., a market test) at a known cost, and *then* decide.

**Sequential pattern:**

1. **Stage 1 Decision:** Buy test ₹ $C$, or Do not buy.
2. **Chance:** Test result is **Positive** or **Negative** with prior probabilities.
3. **Stage 2 Decision:** Launch or Don't Launch — based on the test result.
4. **Payoffs** at tips → roll back stage 2 → roll back stage 1.

The roll-back must be performed **in the reverse order of the decisions** (i.e., starting from the *rightmost* decision node and moving leftward).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Decision-Tree Architecture (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> The figure below is a *generic* block-functional architecture of a decision tree. Decision nodes are drawn as rectangles and chance nodes as circles, in line with standard KTU/OR convention. All node IDs are alphanumeric to avoid Mermaid reserved-keyword conflicts.

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["DECISION NODE D0: Choose Action"]:::dec
    aAct["Action A"]:::act
    bAct["Action B"]:::act
    cAct["No Action / Status Quo"]:::act
    c1["CHANCE NODE C1: State of Nature under A"]:::chance
    c2["CHANCE NODE C2: State of Nature under B"]:::chance
    favA["PAYOFF: Favourable outcome under A (X_AF)"]:::pay
    unfA["PAYOFF: Unfavourable outcome under A (X_AU)"]:::pay
    favB["PAYOFF: Favourable outcome under B (X_BF)"]:::pay
    unfB["PAYOFF: Unfavourable outcome under B (X_BU)"]:::pay
    sqTip["PAYOFF: Status Quo payoff (0)"]:::pay

    root --> aAct
    root --> bAct
    root --> cAct
    aAct --> c1
    bAct --> c2
    cAct --> sqTip
    c1 -->|p = 0.6| favA
    c1 -->|p = 0.4| unfA
    c2 -->|p = 0.6| favB
    c2 -->|p = 0.4| unfB

    classDef dec fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef chance fill:#c97b0a,stroke:#7a4a05,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef act fill:#d9e7f5,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:1px
    classDef pay fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#0b3d0b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The square (root) is a **decision node**. Each circle (C1, C2) is a **chance node** with two probabilistic branches. The green triangles (favA, unfA, …) are **terminal payoffs**. To solve, compute EMV at C1 and C2, then choose the higher-EMV action at the root.

## 4.2 Roll-Back Process Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    st1["Step 1: Draw Tree Left to Right"]:::act
    st2["Step 2: Assign Probabilities to Chance Branches"]:::act
    st3["Step 3: Insert Payoffs at Terminal Nodes"]:::act
    st4["Step 4: Compute EMV at Each Chance Node"]:::chance
    st5["Step 5: Select Best Alternative at Each Decision Node"]:::dec
    st6["Step 6: Continue Until Root - Optimal Decision Found"]:::dec

    st1 --> st2 --> st3 --> st4 --> st5 --> st6

    classDef act fill:#d9e7f5,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:1px
    classDef chance fill:#c97b0a,stroke:#7a4a05,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dec fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 EVPI Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    noInfo["EMV with NO Information = EMV_star"]:::chance
    perfInfo["EVwPI = Sum p_j times Best Payoff in State j"]:::chance
    evpi["EVPI = EVwPI minus EMV_star"]:::dec
    bound["Maximum amount worth paying for perfect information"]:::pay

    noInfo --> evpi
    perfInfo --> evpi
    evpi --> bound

    classDef chance fill:#c97b0a,stroke:#7a4a05,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dec fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pay fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#0b3d0b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a decision tree. List its main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *decision tree* is a graphical model representing a sequence of decisions and chance events, with associated probabilities and payoffs, used to identify the optimal course of action under risk (1 mark). **Main components (2 marks):**
1. **Decision nodes** (squares) – points of choice for the decision-maker.
2. **Chance nodes** (circles) – points where an uncontrollable event occurs with given probabilities.
3. **Branches** – arcs representing either an action or a probabilistic outcome.
4. **Payoffs / outcomes** – monetary (or utility) values shown at terminal nodes.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the formula for the Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI) and explain its managerial significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$EVPI = EVwPI - EMV^{*} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$
where $EVwPI = \sum_j p_j \cdot (\text{best payoff in state } j)$ and $EMV^{*}$ is the optimal EMV without additional information (1 mark).

**Significance (1 mark):** EVPI is the **maximum amount a rational decision-maker should be willing to pay** to obtain *perfect* information about the state of nature. It also serves as the **upper bound** on the value of any sample or survey information.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

A pharmaceutical company must decide whether to launch a new drug *immediately*, *test-market first*, or *abandon the project*. The market response can be **Favourable (p = 0.55)** or **Unfavourable (p = 0.45)**. The market test costs **₹ 3 lakhs** and is **70 % reliable** (i.e., $P(\text{Positive} \mid \text{Favourable}) = 0.7$, $P(\text{Negative} \mid \text{Unfavourable}) = 0.7$).

| Scenario | Payoff (₹ lakhs) |
|:---|---:|
| Launch & Favourable | +40 |
| Launch & Unfavourable | −10 |
| Test, then Launch if Positive | depends on test |
| Test, then Don't Launch if Negative | 0 |
| Abandon | 0 |

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Construct the decision tree, compute all EMVs, and determine the optimal decision.**

**Step 1 — Posterior probabilities via Bayes' theorem.**

$P(\text{Positive}) = (0.7)(0.55) + (0.3)(0.45) = 0.385 + 0.135 = 0.520$

$P(\text{Negative}) = (0.3)(0.55) + (0.7)(0.45) = 0.165 + 0.315 = 0.480$

**Step 2 — Stage-2 decisions (after seeing test result).**

If test is **Positive**:
- Launch: $(0.385 / 0.520)(\text{+40}) + (0.135 / 0.520)(\text{−10})$
- $= (0.7404)(40) + (0.2596)(-10)$
- $= 29.615 - 2.596 = +27.02$ lakhs
- Don't Launch: 0 → **Choose Launch**.

If test is **Negative**:
- Launch: $(0.165 / 0.480)(40) + (0.315 / 0.480)(-10)$
- $= (0.3438)(40) + (0.6562)(-10)$
- $= 13.750 - 6.562 = +7.19$ lakhs
- Don't Launch: 0 → **Choose Launch**.

**Step 3 — Stage-1 decision (Test vs. No-test).**

EMV of **Launch immediately (no test)**:

$$EMV_{Launch} = (0.55)(+40) + (0.45)(-10) = 22 - 4.50 = 17.50 \text{ lakhs}$$

EMV of **Test-then-Launch** (since both test outcomes lead to launch):

$$EMV_{Test} = (0.520)(27.02) + (0.480)(7.19) - 3.00 \text{ (test cost)}$$

$$EMV_{Test} = 14.05 + 3.45 - 3.00 = 14.50 \text{ lakhs}$$

EMV of **Abandon** = 0.

**Decision (7 marks):** $\max(17.50, 14.50, 0) = 17.50$. **Launch immediately without testing** is optimal because the test is imperfect and its cost (₹ 3 L) exceeds its information value.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Find the EVPI and decide whether a *perfect* market survey should be commissioned.**

**Step 1 — EVwPI.**

- If **Favourable** (p = 0.55): best payoff is +40 (Launch).
- If **Unfavourable** (p = 0.45): best payoff is 0 (Abandon).

$$EVwPI = (0.55)(40) + (0.45)(0) = 22.00 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 2 — EMV\* from the optimal decision above.**

$$EMV^{*} = 17.50 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 3 — EVPI.**

$$EVPI = EVwPI - EMV^{*} = 22.00 - 17.50 = 4.50 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Conclusion (7 marks):** A perfect market survey is **worth at most ₹ 4.50 lakhs**. If a real survey can be commissioned for less than this amount *and* is sufficiently reliable, it is worth buying; otherwise, the firm should **launch immediately**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

A construction company can either **Bid (B)** for a new contract or **Decline (D)**. The contract may be **Awarded (p = 0.4)** or **Not Awarded (p = 0.6)**. If awarded, the project will yield a profit of **+₹ 50 lakhs**, but if the cost estimate is wrong the company incurs a loss of **−₹ 20 lakhs** (probability of cost overrun given award = 0.3). If not awarded, payoff is 0.

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Draw the decision tree, calculate the EMVs, and recommend the optimal action.**

**Step 1 — Compute EMV of Bidding.**

If **Awarded (p = 0.4):**
- No overrun (p = 0.7): +50
- Overrun (p = 0.3): −20
- Conditional EMV = $(0.7)(50) + (0.3)(-20) = 35 - 6 = +29$ lakhs

If **Not Awarded (p = 0.6):** payoff = 0

$$EMV_{Bid} = (0.4)(+29) + (0.6)(0) = 11.60 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 2 — EMV of Declining.**

$$EMV_{Decline} = 0$$

**Decision (7 marks):** $\max(11.60, 0) = 11.60$. **Bid for the contract.**

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Compute the EOL for each action, the minimum EOL, and verify the identity $EMV^{*} + \min(EOL) = EVwPI$.**

**Step 1 — Opportunity-Loss (Regret) Table.**

The two possible payoffs of Bid are $+50$ (no overrun) and $-20$ (overrun). The payoff of Decline is always 0.

| State | Prob. | Payoff Bid | Payoff Decline | Column Max | Regret Bid | Regret Decline |
|:---|:---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Awarded-No Overrun | 0.4 × 0.7 = 0.28 | 50 | 0 | 50 | 0 | 50 |
| Awarded-Overrun | 0.4 × 0.3 = 0.12 | −20 | 0 | 0 | 20 | 0 |
| Not Awarded | 0.6 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 2 — Expected Opportunity Loss.**

$$EOL_{Bid} = (0.28)(0) + (0.12)(20) + (0.6)(0) = 2.40 \text{ lakhs}$$

$$EOL_{Decline} = (0.28)(50) + (0.12)(0) + (0.6)(0) = 14.00 \text{ lakhs}$$

$$\min(EOL) = 2.40 \text{ (Bid)}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the identity.**

- $EMV^{*} = 11.60$ (from part a)
- $EVwPI = (0.28)(50) + (0.12)(0) + (0.6)(0) = 14.00$ lakhs
- $EMV^{*} + \min(EOL) = 11.60 + 2.40 = 14.00 = EVwPI$ ✓ **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the test cost** in the *Test-then-Decide* branch — costs must always be deducted from the EMV of the testing branch.
> 2. **Mixing up prior and posterior probabilities** when Bayes' theorem is required. Posterior probabilities apply *after* a test, prior probabilities apply *before*.
> 3. **Choosing the wrong branch** at the decision node (some students take the *lower* EMV). Always select the **maximum EMV** for profit problems and the **minimum EOL** for loss problems.
> 4. **Forgetting the double-slash pruning symbol** on inferior branches — examiners award marks for explicitly showing the rejected alternatives.
> 5. **Not stating the optimal decision in words** — always conclude with a *single-sentence managerial recommendation*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **decision tree** = decision nodes (squares) + chance nodes (circles) + branches + payoffs (triangles).
- [x] **EMV (Expected Monetary Value)** = $\sum p_i X_i$ — computed at every chance node.
- [x] At a **decision node**, choose the alternative with the **highest EMV** (or lowest EOL).
- [x] **Roll-back** is performed **right-to-left**, decision-by-decision.
- [x] **EVwPI** = expected value *if* the state of nature were known with certainty.
- [x] **EVPI** = $EVwPI - EMV^{*}$ = the **maximum** amount worth paying for *perfect* information.
- [x] **EVPI is an UPPER BOUND** on the value of any sample / survey information.
- [x] **EOL (Regret)** is computed from the regret table, where each cell = column maximum − cell value.
- [x] **Fundamental identity** (verify in every Part B answer): $EMV^{*} + \min(EOL) = EVwPI$.
- [x] **Probabilities at every chance node must sum to 1.0.**
- [x] Always subtract **information-acquisition costs** before comparing the test branch with the no-test branch.
- [x] Use **Bayes' theorem** when test reliability (sensitivity / specificity) is given.
- [x] A **risk-averse** decision-maker may prefer a lower-EMV alternative if it has **lower variance** (use Coefficient of Variation $CV = \sigma / EMV$).
- [x] The **final answer must be a managerial recommendation in words**, not just a number.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line takeaway for the KTU exam hall:** *Draw the tree → Assign probabilities & payoffs → Roll back from right to left → Choose the maximum EMV → Compute EVPI as the upper bound on the value of information → Always verify with $EMV^{*} + \min(EOL) = EVwPI$.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EMV Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET612 - QUANTITATIVE TECHNIQUES FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** EMV Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Expected Monetary Value (EMV) Method** is a quantitative decision-making technique used under conditions of **risk**, where the decision-maker can assign numerical probabilities to each possible *state of nature* (future event). The EMV of an alternative is the **probability-weighted average** of all the payoffs (monetary outcomes) associated with that alternative.

Mathematically, for an alternative $A_i$ with $n$ possible states of nature $S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n$, the EMV is given by:

$$EMV(A_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot X_{ij}$$

where $P(S_j)$ is the probability of state $S_j$ occurring, and $X_{ij}$ is the payoff (profit or loss) when alternative $A_i$ is chosen and state $S_j$ occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Decision Theory):**
> The EMV method is a cornerstone of *Decision Making Under Risk*. It assumes the decision-maker is **risk-neutral** (i.e., indifferent to the variability of outcomes, caring only about the long-run average). For risk-averse or risk-seeking decision-makers, supplementary tools like **Utility Theory** are used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Used in KTU Valuated Papers:**
> - **Alternative ($A_i$):** A course of action available to the decision-maker.
> - **State of Nature ($S_j$):** An uncontrollable future event that affects the payoff.
> - **Payoff ($X_{ij}$):** Monetary outcome (profit, cost, or loss) for choosing $A_i$ under $S_j$.
> - **Risk:** States of nature have known objective or subjective probabilities.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Casino Gambler" Analogy** 🎰
>
> Imagine you are at a casino deciding between two slot machines. Machine A pays ₹1,000 with probability 0.1 and ₹0 with probability 0.9. Machine B pays ₹200 with probability 0.5 and ₹0 with probability 0.5.
>
> - **EMV(Machine A)** = $0.1 \times 1000 + 0.9 \times 0 = ₹100$
> - **EMV(Machine B)** = $0.5 \times 200 + 0.5 \times 0 = ₹100$
>
> Both have the same EMV, but Machine A is riskier! A rational EMV maximizer is **indifferent** between them. A risk-averse person would choose Machine B. This illustrates the **central idea**: EMV tells you the *expected* long-run average, not the *guaranteed* short-term result.
>
> **Engineer's Analogy:** A civil engineer choosing between two foundation designs — one cheap but risky in loose soil, one expensive but reliable. EMV lets them quantify "risk" in rupees and make a defensible choice.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Probability values** are dimensionless, lying in $[0, 1]$ with the sum equal to **1**.
> - **Payoff units** are typically **₹ (Rupees), ₹ Lakhs, or ₹ Crores** in KTU exam questions.
> - A perfectly valid alternative has $EMV \geq 0$ (for profit-oriented problems).
> - **EMV Criterion:** Choose the alternative with the **maximum** EMV (profit problems) or the **minimum** EMV (cost/loss problems).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind the EMV Method (Step-by-Step Logic Tree)

The operational logic of the EMV method can be broken down into **6 structured steps**:

- **Step 1 – Identify the Decision:** Clearly state the problem to be solved.
- **Step 2 – Enumerate Alternatives ($A_1, A_2, \dots, A_m$):** List every feasible course of action open to the decision-maker.
- **Step 3 – Identify States of Nature ($S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n$):** Enumerate all possible future events that are outside the decision-maker's control but affect outcomes.
- **Step 4 – Assign Probabilities $P(S_j)$:** Either from historical data (objective), expert judgement (subjective), or Bayesian updates.
- **Step 5 – Construct the Payoff Matrix:** Tabulate the payoff $X_{ij}$ for each (Alternative, State) pair. Verify $\sum P(S_j) = 1$.
- **Step 6 – Compute EMV for Each Alternative and Apply the Decision Rule:** Select the alternative with the maximum (or minimum, for cost problems) EMV.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind EMV:**
> The Law of Large Numbers guarantees that if the same decision is repeated many times, the average outcome converges to the EMV. This is why a rational, risk-neutral decision-maker with repeated exposure finds EMV optimal.

### 2.2 Related Decision-Making Metrics (Derived from EMV Framework)

In the same KTU Module 1 framework, three related metrics extend the EMV analysis:

1. **Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL)** – Regret-based criterion.
2. **Expected Value with Perfect Information (EVwPI)** – Best outcome under certainty.
3. **Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)** – Maximum price the decision-maker should pay for perfect information.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|-------------|-------------|-------------------|
| Expected Monetary Value | $EMV(A_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot X_{ij}$ | Payoff in ₹; Probability is dimensionless |
| Decision Rule (Profit) | Choose $A_i^*$ such that $EMV(A_i^*) = \max_i EMV(A_i)$ | Risk-neutral decision-maker |
| Decision Rule (Cost) | Choose $A_i^*$ such that $EMV(A_i^*) = \min_i EMV(A_i)$ | For minimization problems |
| Opportunity Loss | $L_{ij} = \vert X_{j}^{*} - X_{ij} \vert$, where $X_{j}^{*} = \max_i X_{ij}$ | Difference between best & actual payoff under $S_j$ |
| Expected Opportunity Loss | $EOL(A_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot L_{ij}$ | Choose $A_i$ with minimum EOL |
| Expected Value with Perfect Info | $EVwPI = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot X_{j}^{*}$ | $X_{j}^{*}$ = best payoff in column $j$ |
| Expected Value of Perfect Info | $EVPI = EVwPI - EMV^{*}$ | Always $\geq 0$ |
| Verification Identity | $EMV(A_i) + EOL(A_i) = EVwPI$ | Holds for every alternative $A_i$ |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Mark-Saver Tip:** In KTU valuation, students often confuse EOL minimization with EMV maximization. Remember: *they yield the **same optimal decision** when the payoff table is correctly constructed*. Use EOL only if the question explicitly asks for it.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Choosing between foundation types given soil-test probability data.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Selecting manufacturing processes with known defect-rate probabilities.
- **Industrial Engineering:** Capital budgeting and machine replacement decisions.
- **Software Project Management:** Choosing development strategies with estimated probability of success.
- **Supply Chain Management:** Sourcing decisions with known demand probability distributions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Standard):**
> A startup must decide whether to build a **Large Plant (A1)**, a **Small Plant (A2)**, or **No Plant (A3)**. Market research indicates two possible future states: **High Demand (S1)** with probability **0.6**, and **Low Demand (S2)** with probability **0.4**. The payoff matrix (profits in ₹ Lakhs) is given below. Using the EMV method, determine the optimal decision. Also compute EOL, EVwPI, and EVPI.

**Payoff Matrix:**

| Alternative $\downarrow$ \ State $\rightarrow$ | High Demand $S_1$ ($P=0.6$) | Low Demand $S_2$ ($P=0.4$) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A1: Large Plant** | **200** | $-100$ |
| **A2: Small Plant** | **100** | **50** |
| **A3: No Plant** | **0** | **0** |

---

### 3.2 Step 1 — Compute EMV for Each Alternative

> [!TIP]
> **Logic:** Multiply each payoff in a row by the probability of its column, then sum the products across the row.

**For Alternative A1 (Large Plant):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV(A_1) &= P(S_1) \cdot X_{11} + P(S_2) \cdot X_{12} \\
&= 0.6 \times 200 + 0.4 \times (-100) \\
&= 120 + (-40) \\
&= 80 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Alternative A2 (Small Plant):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV(A_2) &= P(S_1) \cdot X_{21} + P(S_2) \cdot X_{22} \\
&= 0.6 \times 100 + 0.4 \times 50 \\
&= 60 + 20 \\
&= 80 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Alternative A3 (No Plant):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV(A_3) &= P(S_1) \cdot X_{31} + P(S_2) \cdot X_{32} \\
&= 0.6 \times 0 + 0.4 \times 0 \\
&= 0 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** Choose **A1 (Large Plant)** *or* **A2 (Small Plant)** since both give the maximum EMV of **₹80 Lakhs**. (A tie is a classic KTU trap — see Section 5 warning.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a tie occurs, KTU expects students to break the tie using **secondary criteria**: minimum EOL, minimum variance, or maximum minimum-payoff (Maximin).

---

### 3.3 Step 2 — Construct the Opportunity Loss (Regret) Matrix

> [!TIP]
> **Logic:** For each column (state), find the maximum payoff. Then for each cell, compute $L_{ij} = \max(X_{ij}) - X_{ij}$.

**Column-wise maxima:**

- Under $S_1$ (High Demand): $\max(200, 100, 0) = 200$ (Choose A1)
- Under $S_2$ (Low Demand): $\max(-100, 50, 0) = 50$ (Choose A2)

**Regret Matrix:**

| Alternative $\downarrow$ \ State $\rightarrow$ | High Demand $S_1$ | Low Demand $S_2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A1: Large Plant** | $200 - 200 = 0$ | $50 - (-100) = 150$ |
| **A2: Small Plant** | $200 - 100 = 100$ | $50 - 50 = 0$ |
| **A3: No Plant** | $200 - 0 = 200$ | $50 - 0 = 50$ |

---

### 3.4 Step 3 — Compute EOL for Each Alternative

**For Alternative A1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOL(A_1) &= 0.6 \times 0 + 0.4 \times 150 \\
&= 0 + 60 \\
&= 60 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Alternative A2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOL(A_2) &= 0.6 \times 100 + 0.4 \times 0 \\
&= 60 + 0 \\
&= 60 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Alternative A3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOL(A_3) &= 0.6 \times 200 + 0.4 \times 50 \\
&= 120 + 20 \\
&= 140 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Tie-Breaker via EOL:** $EOL(A_1) = EOL(A_2) = 60$ Lakhs (minimum). Both are equally optimal under EOL.

> [!NOTE]
> **Consistency Check:** $EMV(A_i) + EOL(A_i) = EVwPI$ for every $i$. Let us verify this after computing EVwPI.

---

### 3.5 Step 4 — Compute EVwPI and EVPI

> [!TIP]
> **Logic:** With perfect information, you would always pick the column-maximum. Compute the probability-weighted average of those maxima.

**For each state, choose the best alternative:**

- Under $S_1$: Best is A1 with payoff **200**
- Under $S_2$: Best is A2 with payoff **50**

**Expected Value with Perfect Information:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EVwPI &= P(S_1) \cdot X_{1}^{*} + P(S_2) \cdot X_{2}^{*} \\
&= 0.6 \times 200 + 0.4 \times 50 \\
&= 120 + 20 \\
&= 140 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Expected Value of Perfect Information:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EVPI &= EVwPI - \max_i EMV(A_i) \\
&= 140 - 80 \\
&= 60 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The startup should pay **at most ₹60 Lakhs** for perfect market information. Paying more is irrational.

---

### 3.6 Step 5 — Verification Identity

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV(A_1) + EOL(A_1) &= 80 + 60 = 140 = EVwPI \quad \checkmark \\
EMV(A_2) + EOL(A_2) &= 80 + 60 = 140 = EVwPI \quad \checkmark \\
EMV(A_3) + EOL(A_3) &= 0 + 140 = 140 = EVwPI \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This identity is a **powerful self-check** in KTU exams. Always verify your computations using this relationship to catch arithmetic errors and gain partial marks.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Alternative | EMV (₹ Lakhs) | EOL (₹ Lakhs) | Verdict |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A1: Large Plant | 80 | 60 | Tied Optimal |
| A2: Small Plant | 80 | 60 | Tied Optimal |
| A3: No Plant | 0 | 140 | Rejected |

| Metric | Value (₹ Lakhs) | Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| $EMV^{*}$ | 80 | Best expected profit without perfect info |
| $EVwPI$ | 140 | Best expected profit with perfect info |
| $EVPI$ | 60 | Max. amount to pay for perfect information |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 EMV Decision-Making Block Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **algorithmic flow** of the EMV method, mapping the sequential interactions between each computational stage:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Start: Identify Decision Problem] --> A2[Enumerate Alternatives Ai]
    A2 --> A3[Enumerate States of Nature Sj]
    A3 --> A4[Assign Probabilities P Sj]
    A4 --> A5[Construct Payoff Matrix Xij]
    A5 --> A6{Probabilities Sum to 1?}
    A6 -->|No| A7[Revise Probabilities]
    A7 --> A4
    A6 -->|Yes| A8[Compute EMV for each Ai]
    A8 --> A9[Identify Maximum EMV]
    A9 --> A10[Select Optimal Alternative Ai Star]
    A10 --> A11{Tie Detected?}
    A11 -->|Yes| A12[Apply Secondary Criterion EOL or Variance]
    A12 --> A10
    A11 -->|No| A13[Compute Regret Matrix]
    A13 --> A14[Compute EOL for each Ai]
    A14 --> A15[Compute EVwPI]
    A15 --> A16[Compute EVPI equals EVwPI minus Max EMV]
    A16 --> A17[Final Output: Optimal Decision and Information Value]
    A17 --> A18[End]
```

### 4.2 Payoff-to-Decision Process Block Diagram

This diagram shows the **functional architecture** of how a raw payoff matrix is transformed into a strategic decision using the EMV framework:

```mermaid
graph LR
    B1[Raw Payoff Matrix Xij] --> B2[Row-wise Probability Weighting]
    B2 --> B3[EMV Vector EMV Ai]
    B3 --> B4[Max Selection Criterion]
    B4 --> B5[Optimal Alternative Ai Star]
    B1 --> B6[Column-wise Maxima Extraction]
    B6 --> B7[Regret Matrix Lij]
    B7 --> B8[EOL Vector EOL Ai]
    B8 --> B9[Min Selection Criterion]
    B9 --> B10[Tie Verification]
    B6 --> B11[Probability Weighting of Maxima]
    B11 --> B12[EVwPI]
    B3 --> B13[Max EMV Extraction]
    B12 --> B14[EVPI equals EVwPI minus Max EMV]
    B13 --> B14
    B5 --> C1[Final Decision Report]
    B10 --> C1
    B14 --> C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each rectangular block represents a computational stage. The `{}`-shaped blocks are conditional decision gates. The flow `graph TD` denotes Top-Down orientation; `graph LR` denotes Left-Right orientation. All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B5`) to comply with Mermaid compilation safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Define the **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** criterion. Under what conditions is it the appropriate tool for decision-making?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Expected Monetary Value of an alternative $A_i$ is the probability-weighted sum of all its possible payoffs:

$$
EMV(A_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot X_{ij}
$$

It is appropriate under conditions of **risk** — when the decision-maker can assign meaningful numerical probabilities to each state of nature, and is **risk-neutral** (i.e., evaluates decisions by expected long-run average outcome). The decision rule is to **select the alternative with the maximum EMV** (for profit problems) or **minimum EMV** (for cost problems). *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for condition, 1 for decision rule.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Distinguish between **Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)** and **Expected Value with Perfect Information (EVwPI)**. State the relationship between them.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **EVwPI** is the expected payoff when the decision-maker has perfect foreknowledge of which state of nature will occur. Mathematically:
$$EVwPI = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot X_{j}^{*}$$
where $X_{j}^{*}$ is the best payoff in column $j$.
- **EVPI** is the *additional* expected value gained by having perfect information over the best EMV without it:
$$EVPI = EVwPI - EMV^{*}$$
EVPI represents the **maximum amount** a rational decision-maker should be willing to pay for perfect information. *(3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 1 Choice Question]**
> A manufacturing company is evaluating three investment alternatives — **A1: Expand Plant**, **A2: Modernize Plant**, **A3: Do Nothing** — under three states of demand: **High ($S_1$, P = 0.3)**, **Medium ($S_2$, P = 0.5)**, **Low ($S_3$, P = 0.2)**. The payoff matrix (profits in ₹ Crores) is:

| Alternative | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A1: Expand | 50 | 30 | $-10$ |
| A2: Modernize | 40 | 35 | 5 |
| A3: Do Nothing | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Determine the optimal decision using the **EMV criterion**. (7 marks)
**(b)** Construct the **Regret (Opportunity Loss) matrix**, compute the **EOL** for each alternative, and verify your answer using the **EVwPI and EVPI** computations. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) EMV Calculations (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV(A_1) &= 0.3 \times 50 + 0.5 \times 30 + 0.2 \times (-10) \\
&= 15 + 15 + (-2) \\
&= 28 \text{ Crores} \\[6pt]
EMV(A_2) &= 0.3 \times 40 + 0.5 \times 35 + 0.2 \times 5 \\
&= 12 + 17.5 + 1 \\
&= 30.5 \text{ Crores} \\[6pt]
EMV(A_3) &= 0.3 \times 0 + 0.5 \times 0 + 0.2 \times 0 \\
&= 0 \text{ Crores}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Optimal Decision:** Choose **A2: Modernize Plant** with maximum EMV of **₹30.5 Crores**. *(7 marks — 2 per row, 1 for conclusion.)*

**(b) EOL, EVwPI, and EVPI (7 marks):**

**Column maxima of the payoff matrix:**
- $\max(50, 40, 0) = 50$ (under $S_1$)
- $\max(30, 35, 0) = 35$ (under $S_2$)
- $\max(-10, 5, 0) = 5$ (under $S_3$)

**Regret Matrix:**

| Alternative | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A1 | $50-50=0$ | $35-30=5$ | $5-(-10)=15$ |
| A2 | $50-40=10$ | $35-35=0$ | $5-5=0$ |
| A3 | $50-0=50$ | $35-0=35$ | $5-0=5$ |

**EOL Calculations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOL(A_1) &= 0.3 \times 0 + 0.5 \times 5 + 0.2 \times 15 = 0 + 2.5 + 3 = 5.5 \text{ Crores} \\
EOL(A_2) &= 0.3 \times 10 + 0.5 \times 0 + 0.2 \times 0 = 3 + 0 + 0 = 3 \text{ Crores} \\
EOL(A_3) &= 0.3 \times 50 + 0.5 \times 35 + 0.2 \times 5 = 15 + 17.5 + 1 = 33.5 \text{ Crores}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision via EOL:** Choose **A2** (minimum EOL = ₹3 Crores). ✓ Consistent with EMV.

**EVwPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EVwPI &= 0.3 \times 50 + 0.5 \times 35 + 0.2 \times 5 \\
&= 15 + 17.5 + 1 \\
&= 33.5 \text{ Crores}
\end{aligned}
$$

**EVPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EVPI &= EVwPI - EMV^{*} = 33.5 - 30.5 = 3 \text{ Crores}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $EMV(A_2) + EOL(A_2) = 30.5 + 3 = 33.5 = EVwPI$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The company should **Modernize the Plant**. Perfect market information is worth at most **₹3 Crores**. *(7 marks — 2 for regret matrix, 2 for EOL, 2 for EVwPI/EVPI, 1 for verification.)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
A retailer must decide between **three product lines**: **A1: Premium**, **A2: Standard**, **A3: Economy**. Market states are **Favourable ($S_1$, P = 0.4)**, **Average ($S_2$, P = 0.4)**, **Unfavourable ($S_3$, P = 0.2)**. Payoffs (₹ Lakhs):

| Alternative | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A1: Premium | 80 | 40 | $-20$ |
| A2: Standard | 50 | 45 | 10 |
| A3: Economy | 20 | 20 | 15 |

**(a)** Apply the EMV criterion to determine the optimal product line. (7 marks)
**(b)** Compute EOL for all alternatives and verify the decision using EVPI. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) EMV (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV(A_1) &= 0.4 \times 80 + 0.4 \times 40 + 0.2 \times (-20) \\
&= 32 + 16 - 4 = 44 \text{ Lakhs} \\[4pt]
EMV(A_2) &= 0.4 \times 50 + 0.4 \times 45 + 0.2 \times 10 \\
&= 20 + 18 + 2 = 40 \text{ Lakhs} \\[4pt]
EMV(A_3) &= 0.4 \times 20 + 0.4 \times 20 + 0.2 \times 15 \\
&= 8 + 8 + 3 = 19 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Optimal Decision:** **A1: Premium** with EMV = **₹44 Lakhs**. *(7 marks.)*

**(b) EOL and EVPI (7 marks):**

**Column maxima:** $S_1 \rightarrow 80$, $S_2 \rightarrow 45$, $S_3 \rightarrow 15$.

**Regret Matrix:**

| Alternative | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A1 | 0 | 5 | 35 |
| A2 | 30 | 0 | 5 |
| A3 | 60 | 25 | 0 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOL(A_1) &= 0.4 \times 0 + 0.4 \times 5 + 0.2 \times 35 = 0 + 2 + 7 = 9 \text{ Lakhs} \\
EOL(A_2) &= 0.4 \times 30 + 0.4 \times 0 + 0.2 \times 5 = 12 + 0 + 1 = 13 \text{ Lakhs} \\
EOL(A_3) &= 0.4 \times 60 + 0.4 \times 25 + 0.2 \times 0 = 24 + 10 + 0 = 34 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum EOL** = 9 Lakhs at A1 ✓ (Matches EMV).

$$
\begin{aligned}
EVwPI &= 0.4 \times 80 + 0.4 \times 45 + 0.2 \times 15 = 32 + 18 + 3 = 53 \text{ Lakhs} \\
EVPI &= 53 - 44 = 9 \text{ Lakhs} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $EMV(A_1) + EOL(A_1) = 44 + 9 = 53 = EVwPI$ ✓ *(7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by probability:** Students often sum the payoffs in a row without weighting. This is a fatal error; full marks are withheld.
> 2. **Sign errors in loss payoffs:** Negative payoffs (losses) must be carried with their signs into the EMV sum. A common mistake is treating $-10$ as $+10$.
> 3. **Probability sum not equal to 1:** If the probabilities don't sum to 1, KTU expects a remark; in some problems, students must first *normalize* the probabilities.
> 4. **Tie-breaking:** If two EMVs are equal, do NOT silently pick one. State the tie and use EOL, variance, or Maximin to resolve it.
> 5. **Regret matrix construction:** Students often compute $L_{ij} = X_{ij} - \max(X_{ij})$, which gives a *negative* matrix. The correct form is $L_{ij} = \max(X_{ij}) - X_{ij}$ (always $\geq 0$).
> 6. **Unit consistency:** Always write the final answer with units (Lakhs/Crores). KTU deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Pin This Before the Exam:**
>
> ✅ **EMV Definition:** Weighted average payoff, weights = state probabilities.
> ✅ **Core Formula:** $EMV(A_i) = \sum P(S_j) \cdot X_{ij}$ — memorize cold.
> ✅ **Decision Rule (Profit):** Choose the alternative with the **maximum** EMV.
> ✅ **Decision Rule (Cost):** Choose the alternative with the **minimum** EMV.
> ✅ **Assumption:** Decision-maker is **risk-neutral** and has known $P(S_j)$.
> ✅ **Regret / Opportunity Loss:** $L_{ij} = \max(X_{ij}) - X_{ij}$ per column.
> ✅ **EOL Formula:** $EOL(A_i) = \sum P(S_j) \cdot L_{ij}$ — choose **minimum**.
> ✅ **EVwPI:** $\sum P(S_j) \cdot (\text{column max in state } S_j)$.
> ✅ **EVPI:** $EVwPI - EMV^{*}$ — always non-negative.
> ✅ **Identity Check:** $EMV(A_i) + EOL(A_i) = EVwPI$ — use it as a verification tool.
> ✅ **Tie-Breaker Hierarchy:** EOL → Variance → Maximin → Laplace.
> ✅ **Unit Reminder:** Always carry ₹ Lakhs / ₹ Crores in the final answer.
> ✅ **Common KTU Traps:** (i) Sign errors on losses, (ii) probability mis-summation, (iii) missing tie-breaking explanation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EOL Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET612 - QUANTITATIVE TECHNIQUES FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** EOL Method # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **EOL Method** (Economic Order Level / Economic Order Quantity method), popularly known as the **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)** technique or **Wilson's Economic Lot Size Formula**, is a classical inventory management model in operations research used to determine the **fixed optimal order quantity** that a firm should purchase in order to **minimize the sum of ordering costs, holding (carrying) costs, and shortage costs** associated with inventory. In the KTU 2024 scheme for *Quantitative Techniques for Engineers*, the EOL Method is classified under deterministic inventory control models where demand is assumed to be **known, constant, and continuous**, and replenishment is **instantaneous** (i.e., the order arrives in one batch the moment stock is exhausted). Mathematically, EOL is the order quantity at which the **Total Inventory Cost (TIC)** curve attains its **global minimum** — i.e., the point at which the marginal ordering cost exactly equals the marginal holding cost. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Learn Verbatim):** > *"Economic Order Quantity (EOL) is the quantity of inventory to be ordered at each reorder point which minimizes the total variable costs associated with inventory — namely, the ordering cost, the holding cost, and the shortage cost."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation Imagine you run a small grocery shop and sell **sanitary napkins**. Every month you sell exactly **1,000 packs** (constant demand). You have two choices every time you place an order: - **Order too frequently** (say, 50 packs at a time) → You place **20 orders per month**. Each order has a delivery/transport cost (say ₹200). You spend ₹4,000 just on delivery, and your storeroom never overflows. - **Order too much at once** (say, 5,000 packs) → You place **1 order in 5 months**, saving transport money, but now your storeroom is bursting. You pay rent, pest-control, and risk expiry/damage (carrying cost). The **EOL** is the *Goldilocks* quantity — **not too small, not too large** — that perfectly balances these two opposing cost forces so that the **total cost reaches its absolute minimum**. > [!NOTE] > **The Two Competing Forces in EOL:** > 1. **Ordering Cost** → Wants you to order **LESS frequently** in **LARGE quantities** (favours large Q). > 2. **Holding (Carrying) Cost** → Wants you to order **MORE frequently** in **SMALL quantities** (favours small Q). > 3. **EOL = The truce point where these two forces balance.** ⚖️ ## 1.3 Assumptions of the Basic EOL Model | # | Assumption | Engineering Interpretation | |---|---|---| | 1 | Demand is **deterministic** and constant over time | Production planning is fully predictable | | 2 | Replenishment (lead time) is **instantaneous** or constant | The supplier delivers the entire batch the moment it is ordered | | 3 | Order quantity is **fixed and constant** per cycle | No quantity discounts, no backorders | | 4 | Purchase price per unit is **constant** (no discounts) | Cost per item does not depend on volume | | 5 | Holding cost is a **linear function** of average inventory | Charged as a fixed % of unit cost per year | | 6 | Ordering cost is **fixed per order** | Independent of order size | | 7 | No **stock-outs** are permitted | Service level = 100% | | 8 | The planning horizon is **infinite / continuous** | Long-run steady-state operation | ## 1.4 Standard Symbols & Units > [!NOTE] > **Universal Notation used in KTU Board Papers:** > - $D$ → Annual demand (units/year) — **bold unit: units/year** > - $Q$ or $Q^*$ → Order quantity per order (units) — *the variable we solve for* > - $S$ or $C_o$ → Ordering cost per order (₹/order) > - $H$ or $C_h$ → Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year) > - $C$ → Unit purchase cost (₹/unit) > - $L$ → Lead time (days or weeks) > - $N$ → Number of orders per year (dimensionless) > - $T_c$ → Total annual inventory cost (₹/year) > - $t$ → Cycle time (time between two consecutive orders) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Cost Behaviour Analysis (The "Why" Behind the Curve) To deeply understand the EOL derivation, you must understand the *behaviour* of the three cost components as $Q$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$: ### A. Annual Ordering Cost (AOC) $$\text{AOC} = \frac{D}{Q} \times S$$ - **Behaviour**: **Hyperbolic decay** — As $Q$ increases, the number of orders $\left(\frac{D}{Q}\right)$ falls, so ordering cost plummets. - **Asymptote**: Approaches **0** as $Q \to \infty$ (you order once a year). - **Asymptote**: Approaches $\infty$ as $Q \to 0$ (you order infinitely many tiny orders). ### B. Annual Holding / Carrying Cost (AHC) $$\text{AHC} = \frac{Q}{2} \times H$$ - **Behaviour**: **Linear growth** — As $Q$ increases, the average inventory level $\left(\frac{Q}{2}\right)$ rises, so holding cost increases linearly. - **Origin**: Equals **0** when $Q = 0$ (no inventory to hold). - **Growth**: Approaches $\infty$ as $Q \to \infty$ (storeroom is always full). ### C. Annual Total Variable Cost (ATC) — *the optimization target* $$\text{ATC} = \text{AOC} + \text{AHC} = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{HQ}{2}$$ This is a **U-shaped convex function** in $Q$, which guarantees the existence of a unique global minimum — our Economic Order Level. ## 2.2 The Master Derivation Logic (Why does AOC = AHC at EOL?) The mathematical proof of the optimality condition is elegant: 1. Total cost $T_c(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{HQ}{2}$ is **convex** in $Q$. 2. To find the minimum, take the **first derivative** and set it to **zero**: $$\frac{dT_c}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$ 3. Solving yields $Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$, hence: $$\boxed{Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}$$ 4. Substituting $Q^*$ back, note that the two terms $\frac{DS}{Q^*}$ and $\frac{HQ^*}{2}$ become **exactly equal** — proving that **at the optimal order level, the annual ordering cost equals the annual holding cost**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Universal Truth (Board Favourite Property):** > *At EOL, Annual Ordering Cost = Annual Holding Cost = $\sqrt{DSH} \div 1$, and the minimum Total Variable Cost is $\sqrt{2DSH}$.* ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | KTU Frequent Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Economic Order Level (EOL / EOQ)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | units | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 2 | Optimal number of orders per year | $N^* = \dfrac{D}{Q^*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{DH}{2S}}$ | orders/year | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 3 | Optimal cycle time (in years) | $T^* = \dfrac{Q^*}{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2S}{DH}}$ | years | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 4 | Optimal cycle time (in months) | $T_m^* = 12 \sqrt{\dfrac{2S}{DH}}$ | months | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 5 | Reorder Point (ROP) | $\text{ROP} = D \times L$ | units | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 6 | Average inventory | $\bar{I} = \dfrac{Q^*}{2}$ | units | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 7 | Minimum Total Variable Cost | $T_c^{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$ | ₹/year | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 8 | Ordering cost at EOL | $\text{AOC}^* = \sqrt{DSH \div 1}$ | ₹/year | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 9 | Holding cost at EOL | $\text{AHC}^* = \sqrt{DSH \div 1}$ | ₹/year | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 10 | Total Annual Cost (incl. purchase) | $T_{tot} = DC + \sqrt{2DSH}$ | ₹/year | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 11 | Production Order Quantity (POQ) | $Q_p^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H} \cdot \dfrac{p}{p-d}}$ | units | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 12 | EOQ with backorders | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_c + C_o}{C_c}}$ | units | ⭐⭐ | | 13 | EOQ with quantity discount | Trial-and-error on price breaks | — | ⭐⭐ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Pipe Notation:** In the table above, the symbol `\div` is used wherever an absolute-value expression or ratio would normally require the vertical bar `|`. In LaTeX, always prefer `\vert`, `\mid`, or `\div` to maintain table integrity. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications | Industry | Application of EOL | |---|---| | **Manufacturing** | Raw material procurement, fastener bolts, lubricant drums | | **Retail / FMCG** | Replenishing fast-moving consumer goods | | **Pharmaceuticals** | Drug inventory at hospitals (life-saving drugs) | | **Automotive** | Spare parts warehousing at service centres | | **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart)** | Bin replenishment at FCs (Fulfilment Centres) | | **Civil Engineering** | Cement, steel, aggregate ordering on construction sites | | **Aerospace (ISRO, Boeing)** | Component-level EOQ for MRO operations | | **Agriculture / FPOs** | Seed, fertilizer, and pesticide bulk ordering | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The EOL model is the *intellectual parent* of **MRP (Material Requirements Planning)**, **JIT (Just-In-Time)** philosophies, and modern **SAP/ERP** inventory modules. Every modern supply chain runs an EOL-like optimization in its backend. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EOL Formula ### Step 0: Define the Objective Function The total variable cost of inventory per year is the sum of the annual ordering cost and the annual holding cost: $$T_c(Q) = \underbrace{\left(\frac{D}{Q}\right) S}_{\text{Ordering cost}} + \underbrace{\left(\frac{Q}{2}\right) H}_{\text{Holding cost}}$$ ### Step 1: Validate Convexity (Second Derivative Test) Take the first derivative: $$\frac{dT_c}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2}$$ Take the second derivative: $$\frac{d^{2}T_c}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \forall Q > 0$$ Since the second derivative is **strictly positive** for all positive $Q$, the function is **strictly convex** → a unique global minimum exists. ### Step 2: Set First Derivative to Zero (First-Order Necessary Condition) $$-\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$ ### Step 3: Isolate $Q$ $$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{DS}{Q^2}$$ Multiply both sides by $Q^2$: $$\frac{H \cdot Q^2}{2} = DS$$ Solve for $Q^2$: $$Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$$ Take the positive square root (since order quantity must be positive): $$\boxed{Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}$$ ### Step 4: Compute Minimum Total Cost Substitute $Q^*$ back into the total cost equation. Using the property $\dfrac{DS}{Q^*} = \dfrac{HQ^*}{2}$ at optimum: $$T_c^{\min} = \frac{DS}{Q^*} + \frac{HQ^*}{2} = 2 \cdot \frac{HQ^*}{2} = H \cdot Q^* = H \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{2DSH}$$ ### Step 5: Verify the Balance Property - Ordering cost at EOL: $\frac{DS}{Q^*} = \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}$ - Holding cost at EOL: $\frac{HQ^*}{2} = \frac{H \sqrt{2DS/H}}{2} = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}$ ✅ Both are equal — confirming the **fundamental EOL balance property**. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example (14-Mark Pattern) > **Problem:** A manufacturing firm uses **10,000 units** of a component annually. The cost of placing one order is **₹200**, and the holding cost per unit per year is **20% of the unit cost**. If the unit cost is **₹50**, determine: > (a) Economic Order Level (EOL) > (b) Optimal number of orders per year > (c) Optimal cycle time in months > (d) Total annual variable cost > (e) Reorder point, given a lead time of 1 month ### Solution: **Given Data:** $$D = 10{,}000 \text{ units/year}, \quad S = ₹200/\text{order}, \quad C = ₹50/\text{unit}, \quad H = 0.20 \times 50 = ₹10/\text{unit/year}, \quad L = 1 \text{ month}$$ **Part (a): EOL** $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{10}} = \sqrt{\frac{4{,}000{,}000}{10}} = \sqrt{400{,}000}$$ $$Q^* = 632.46 \approx 632 \text{ units}$$ **Part (b): Optimal Number of Orders** $$N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{10{,}000}{632.46} = 15.81 \approx 16 \text{ orders per year}$$ **Part (c): Optimal Cycle Time (in months)** $$T_m^* = \frac{12 \times Q^*}{D} = \frac{12 \times 632.46}{10{,}000} = 0.759 \text{ months} \approx 22.76 \text{ days}$$ **Part (d): Total Annual Variable Cost** $$T_c^{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200 \times 10} = \sqrt{40{,}000{,}000} = ₹6{,}324.56/\text{year}$$ If purchase cost is included: $$T_{tot} = DC + T_c^{\min} = (10{,}000)(50) + 6{,}324.56 = ₹5{,}06{,}324.56/\text{year}$$ **Part (e): Reorder Point** $$\text{ROP} = D \times L_{\text{(in years)}} = 10{,}000 \times \frac{1}{12} = 833.33 \approx 834 \text{ units}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of EOL Variants | Variant | Assumptions | EOL Formula | Real-World Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Basic EOL** | No shortages, no discounts, instantaneous replenishment | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Foundational textbook model | | **Production EOL (POQ)** | Finite production rate $p > d$ | $Q_p^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{p-d}}$ | When a factory produces in-house | | **EOL with Backorders** | Permits stockouts, penalty cost $C_o$ | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_c + C_o}{C_c}}$ | When late delivery is acceptable | | **EOL with Quantity Discount** | Price breaks at higher Q | Step-wise test of total cost at each break | When suppliers offer bulk rates | | **EOL with Defectives** | A fraction $\theta$ of each lot is defective | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H(1-\theta)}}$ | Quality-controlled procurement | | **EOL with Safety Stock** | Variable demand | Adds safety stock to ROP | High-uncertainty supply chains | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in KTU papers:** A common 14-mark KTU question asks to *"compare the basic EOL with the production EOL"* or *"derive EOL with backorders."* Master the matrix above to score full marks. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Inventory Level vs. Time (Saw-Tooth Cycle) This diagram shows the classic **saw-tooth inventory profile** under the instantaneous replenishment assumption. ```mermaid graph LR cycleStartA["Cycle Start: Order Q units received"] --> highA["Maximum Inventory = Q units"] highA --> consumeA["Steady Consumption at rate D"] consumeA --> lowA["Inventory reaches zero"] lowA --> orderTriggerA["Reorder Trigger: ROP = D x L"] orderTriggerA --> cycleStartB["Cycle Start: New order Q received"] cycleStartB --> highB["Max Inventory = Q units"] highB --> consumeB["Steady Consumption at rate D"] consumeB --> lowB["Inventory reaches zero"] lowB --> orderTriggerB["Reorder Trigger"] orderTriggerB --> cycleEnd["Cycle Repeats N times per year"] classDef peak fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef trough fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef trigger fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 class highA,highB peak class lowA,lowB,consumeA,consumeB trough class orderTriggerA,orderTriggerB trigger ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** Inventory rises **instantaneously** to its peak $Q$ when an order arrives, then falls at a constant slope (rate of demand $D$). When inventory hits the **Reorder Point (ROP)**, a fresh order is placed. The **cycle time $T = Q/D$** is the horizontal distance between two consecutive peaks. ## 4.2 Cost Behaviour Curves — Why EOL is the Minimum ```mermaid graph TB axisA["Q (Order Quantity)"] --- axisB["Annual Cost in Rupees"] subgraph CostCurves direction LR ordCurve["Ordering Cost = DS / Q
HYPERBOLIC DECAY
Starts HIGH, falls to 0"] holdCurve["Holding Cost = HQ / 2
LINEAR GROWTH
Starts at 0, rises steadily"] totalCurve["Total Cost = Ordering + Holding
U-SHAPED CONVEX CURVE
Minimised at EOL"] end ordCurve --- holdCurve holdCurve --- totalCurve eolPoint["OPTIMAL EOL POINT
Q star = sqrt 2DS / H
Where Ordering = Holding
Total Cost = sqrt 2DSH"]:::eolClass totalCurve --> eolPoint classDef eolClass fill:#00FA9A,stroke:#006400,color:#000,stroke-width:3px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Visual Cue:** If a question asks you to *"draw a graph showing the relationship between ordering cost, holding cost, and total cost versus order size,"* use exactly this hyperbolic-decay + linear-growth = U-shaped-minimum construction. ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the EOL Decision Engine ```mermaid graph TD inputA["Input 1: Annual Demand D"] --> engine inputB["Input 2: Ordering Cost S"] --> engine inputC["Input 3: Holding Cost H"] --> engine inputD["Input 4: Lead Time L"] --> engine engine{"EOL Decision Engine
Mathematical Optimiser"}:::core engine --> outA["Output 1: Optimal Q star
sqrt 2DS / H"] engine --> outB["Output 2: Order Frequency N
D / Q star per year"] engine --> outC["Output 3: Cycle Time T
Q star / D years"] engine --> outD["Output 4: Reorder Point ROP
D x L units"] engine --> outE["Output 5: Minimum Total Cost
sqrt 2DSH rupees"] outA --> actionA["Procurement Order Placed"] outB --> actionB["Vendor Scheduling"] outC --> actionC["Inventory Cycle Planning"] outD --> actionD["Warehouse Trigger Settings"] outE --> actionE["Budgetary Approval"] classDef core fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF,stroke-width:3px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block Diagram Reading Guide:** The four cost/demand/lead-time inputs are **fed** into the central EOL Engine. The engine produces **five operational outputs** that feed directly into the firm's procurement, vendor, and budgeting decisions. In real ERP systems, this engine is implemented in the **MM (Materials Management) module of SAP**. ## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — When to Use EOL vs. Other Models ```mermaid graph TD startQ["Inventory Problem Identified"] --> demandKnownQ{"Is demand known and constant?"} demandKnownQ -- "Yes" --> leadTimeQ{"Is lead time fixed?"} demandKnownQ -- "No - Variable" --> ssModel["Use Safety Stock /
Probabilistic Model"] leadTimeQ -- "Yes" --> discountQ{"Are there quantity discounts?"} leadTimeQ -- "No" --> stModel["Use Stochastic Model"] discountQ -- "No" --> shortageQ{"Are backorders allowed?"} discountQ -- "Yes" --> qdModel["Use EOQ with
Quantity Discount Model"] shortageQ -- "No" --> productionQ{"Is replenishment gradual?"} shortageQ -- "Yes" --> boModel["Use EOQ with
Backorders Model"] productionQ -- "No - Instantaneous" --> finalEOL["USE BASIC EOL
Q star = sqrt 2DS / H"] productionQ -- "Yes - Production rate p" --> poqModel["Use Production Order
Quantity POQ Model"] finalEOL --> answerEOL["Answer with EOL Method"] poqModel --> answerPOQ["Answer with POQ Method"] boModel --> answerBO["Answer with Backorder Model"] qdModel --> answerQD["Answer with Discount Model"] ssModel --> answerSS["Answer with Probabilistic Model"] stModel --> answerST["Answer with Stochastic Model"] classDef final fill:#00FA9A,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef branch fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 class finalEOL,answerEOL final ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define Economic Order Level (EOL). State any two assumptions of the basic EOL model.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (2 Marks):** Economic Order Level (EOL), also called Economic Order Quantity (EOQ), is the fixed quantity of inventory to be ordered each time replenishment is required such that the **sum of annual ordering cost and annual holding cost is minimized**. Mathematically: $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ where $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = ordering cost per order, $H$ = holding cost per unit per year. **Assumptions (1 Mark — any two):** 1. Demand is known, constant, and continuous over the planning horizon. 2. Replenishment is instantaneous — the entire order arrives at once. 3. No quantity discounts and no stock-outs are allowed. 4. Ordering cost is fixed per order; holding cost is linear in average inventory. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is the Reorder Point (ROP)? How is it calculated for a constant demand rate?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Reorder Point (ROP)** is the inventory level at which a fresh order must be placed to **avoid stock-out** during the lead time. For constant demand rate $D$ and constant lead time $L$ (expressed as a fraction of a year): $$\boxed{\text{ROP} = D \times L}$$ If lead time is in months, use $L/12$ of a year; if in days, use $L/365$. For example, if $D = 12{,}000$ units/year and $L = 1$ month, then $\text{ROP} = 12{,}000 \times (1/12) = 1{,}000$ units. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the formula for Economic Order Level (EOL). Show that at the optimum, annual ordering cost equals annual holding cost. **(b) [7 Marks]** A company uses **24,000 units** of a raw material per year. The ordering cost is **₹150 per order** and the holding cost is **₹6 per unit per year**. Calculate: 1. Economic Order Quantity 2. Number of orders per year 3. Time between two consecutive orders (in months) 4. Total annual inventory cost 5. Reorder point, if the procurement lead time is **15 days** --- ### Model Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks] **Step 1: Define the cost function** (1 mark) The total variable cost of inventory per year is: $$T_c(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{HQ}{2}$$ where $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = ordering cost per order, $Q$ = order quantity, $H$ = holding cost per unit per year. **Step 2: First-order optimality condition** (2 marks) Differentiate $T_c$ with respect to $Q$: $$\frac{dT_c}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2}$$ Set the derivative to zero for the optimum: $$-\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{H}{2} = \frac{DS}{Q^2} \Rightarrow Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$$ **Step 3: Solve for Q** (1 mark) $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$ **Step 4: Second-derivative test (convexity proof)** (1 mark) $$\frac{d^2 T_c}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0$$ Since the second derivative is positive, the function is convex and the critical point is a **global minimum**. **Step 5: Prove balance property** (2 marks) At $Q = Q^*$: - Annual ordering cost $= \dfrac{DS}{Q^*} = DS \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{H}{2DS}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{DSH}{2}}$ - Annual holding cost $= \dfrac{HQ^*}{2} = \dfrac{H}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{DSH}{2}}$ ✅ Both are equal, proving the balance property at EOL. #### Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks] **Given:** $D = 24{,}000$ units/yr, $S = ₹150$/order, $H = ₹6$/unit/yr, $L = 15$ days. **Step 1: EOL [2 marks]** $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 150}{6}} = \sqrt{\frac{7{,}200{,}000}{6}} = \sqrt{1{,}200{,}000} = 1{,}095.45 \approx 1{,}095 \text{ units}$$ **Step 2: Number of orders per year [1 mark]** $$N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{24{,}000}{1{,}095.45} = 21.91 \approx 22 \text{ orders/year}$$ **Step 3: Time between orders in months [1.5 marks]** $$T_m^* = \frac{12 \times Q^*}{D} = \frac{12 \times 1{,}095.45}{24{,}000} = 0.5477 \text{ months} \approx 16.43 \text{ days}$$ **Step 4: Total annual variable cost [1.5 marks]** $$T_c^{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 150 \times 6} = \sqrt{43{,}200{,}000} = ₹6{,}571.31/\text{year}$$ **Step 5: Reorder point [1 mark]** Lead time in years: $L = 15/365 = 0.0411$ years $$\text{ROP} = D \times L = 24{,}000 \times \frac{15}{365} = 986.30 \approx 987 \text{ units}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** A factory uses **8,000 components** annually. The unit cost is **₹40** and the holding cost is **25% of the unit cost per year**. The ordering cost is **₹100 per order**. Find the EOL. If the manager wants to avail a quantity discount offering the unit cost at **₹38** for orders of **1,000 units or more**, advise whether the discount should be accepted. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Production Order Quantity (POQ)** model. Derive its formula and state how it differs from the basic EOL model. --- ### Model Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — Numerical with Discount Analysis [7 Marks] **Given:** $D = 8{,}000$ units/yr, $C = ₹40$/unit, $i = 25\%$, $S = ₹100$/order. $$H = 0.25 \times 40 = ₹10/\text{unit/year}$$ **Step 1: EOL at ₹40/unit [2 marks]** $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 100}{10}} = \sqrt{160{,}000} = 400 \text{ units}$$ Since $400 < 1{,}000$, the discount slab is **not** triggered under EOL. The decision now compares total costs: **Step 2: Total cost at $Q = 400$ (no discount) [1.5 marks]** $$T_{tot} = DC + \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{HQ}{2} = (8{,}000)(40) + \frac{(8{,}000)(100)}{400} + \frac{(10)(400)}{2}$$ $$T_{tot} = 3{,}20{,}000 + 2{,}000 + 2{,}000 = ₹3{,}24{,}000/\text{year}$$ **Step 3: Total cost at $Q = 1{,}000$ (with discount, $C = ₹38$, $H = 0.25 \times 38 = ₹9.5$) [2 marks]** $$T_{tot} = (8{,}000)(38) + \frac{(8{,}000)(100)}{1{,}000} + \frac{(9.5)(1{,}000)}{2}$$ $$T_{tot} = 3{,}04{,}000 + 800 + 4{,}750 = ₹3{,}09{,}550/\text{year}$$ **Step 4: Comparison and decision [1.5 marks]** Savings from discount: $3{,}24{,}000 - 3{,}09{,}550 = ₹14{,}450$ per year. ✅ **The discount SHOULD be accepted**, as it reduces total annual cost by ₹14,450 even though holding cost rises. #### Part (b) — POQ Theory [7 Marks] **Definition (2 marks):** The **Production Order Quantity (POQ)** model, also called the **Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)** model, is used when inventory is **built up gradually** rather than instantaneously — i.e., the firm manufactures (or receives) items at a **finite production rate $p$** while simultaneously consuming them at demand rate $d$ (with $p > d$). **Derivation (3 marks):** The maximum inventory level is no longer $Q$, but $Q \cdot (1 - d/p) = Q(p-d)/p$. Hence, average inventory is: $$\bar{I} = \frac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$ The total cost function becomes: $$T_c = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{HQ}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$ Differentiating and setting $dT_c/dQ = 0$: $$Q_p^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H} \cdot \frac{p}{p-d}}$$ **Comparison with basic EOL (2 marks):** | Feature | Basic EOL | Production EOL (POQ) | |---|---|---| | Replenishment | Instantaneous (whole batch arrives at once) | Gradual, at rate $p$ | | Max inventory | $Q$ | $Q(p-d)/p$ | | Formula | $\sqrt{2DS/H}$ | $\sqrt{2DS/H} \cdot \sqrt{p/(p-d)}$ | | Cycle structure | Saw-tooth with vertical rise | Saw-tooth with ramped rise | | Application | External procurement | In-house production | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in EOL Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the discount-aware holding cost** — When a quantity discount changes the unit cost, the holding cost $H$ *also* changes (since $H = i \times C$). Many students recompute $Q^*$ but forget to update $H$. **Penalty: −2 marks.** > 2. **Mixing up lead time units** — KTU commonly gives lead time in *days* or *months*. Always convert to *years* before computing ROP, i.e., $\text{ROP} = D \times (L_{\text{days}}/365)$. **Penalty: −1 mark.** > 3. **Using EOQ when production is gradual** — If the problem says *"the company manufactures at a rate of $p$ units/day,"* the correct model is **POQ, not basic EOL**. **Penalty: −3 to −4 marks** (entire formula wrong). > 4. **Skipping the convexity check** — A complete derivation **must** include $d^2T_c/dQ^2 > 0$ to confirm the minimum. **Penalty: −1 mark.** > 5. **Not stating the balance property** — Examiners love the line *"at EOL, annual ordering cost = annual holding cost."* Omitting it costs 1–2 marks. > 6. **Unit mismatch in $H$** — If $H$ is given as a percentage (e.g., *"20% of unit cost"*), you **must** multiply by the unit cost first. **Penalty: −1 mark.** ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — EOL Method (Must Memorize)** - **Full Name:** Economic Order Level / Economic Order Quantity (Wilson, 1934). - **Objective:** Minimize the sum of ordering cost + holding cost. - **Master Formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$. - **Minimum Total Variable Cost:** $T_c^{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$. - **Balance Property:** At EOL, $\text{AOC}^* = \text{AHC}^* = \sqrt{DSH/2}$. - **Number of Orders / Year:** $N^* = D/Q^* = \sqrt{DH/(2S)}$. - **Cycle Time (years):** $T^* = Q^*/D = \sqrt{2S/(DH)}$. - **Cycle Time (months):** $T_m^* = 12\sqrt{2S/(DH)}$. - **Reorder Point:** $\text{ROP} = D \times L$ (convert $L$ to years). - **Holding Cost Conversion:** $H = i \times C$ where $i$ is the carrying charge rate (decimal). - **POQ Variant:** $Q_p^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H} \cdot \dfrac{p}{p-d}}$ for finite production rate. - **Backorder Variant:** $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_c + C_o}{C_c}}$. - **Discount Strategy:** Always recompute $H$ and $T_{tot}$ at each price break before recommending. - **Convexity Condition:** $d^2T_c/dQ^2 = 2DS/Q^3 > 0$ — always positive → unique minimum. - **Industrial Use:** SAP MM, Oracle ERP, MRP, JIT, supply chain optimization. - **Inventory Profile:** Saw-tooth (instantaneous rise, linear decay) under basic EOL. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Mantra for EOL:** > *"Read the problem carefully → identify the model (Basic / POQ / Backorder / Discount) → list the data with correct units → apply the formula → state the balance property → draw a clean cost curve if a graph is asked → double-check that $H$ is in ₹/unit/year."* ✅

topics.title: MaxiMin Criterion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET612 - QUANTITATIVE TECHNIQUES FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MaxiMin Criterion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MaxiMin Criterion (Wald's Criterion / Pessimism Criterion)**
>
> The **MaxiMin Criterion** is a decision-making rule applied under conditions of **uncertainty** (or complete ignorance) where the decision-maker lacks any probabilistic information regarding the *states of nature*. The decision-maker identifies the **minimum payoff** achievable for every alternative action, and then selects the alternative that yields the **maximum of these minimum payoffs**. Mathematically, it is expressed as:
>
> $$\text{Optimal Decision } (d^*) = \max_{i} \left[ \min_{j} \, a_{ij} \right]$$
>
> where $a_{ij}$ represents the payoff obtained from choosing alternative $i$ when state of nature $j$ occurs.

This criterion was formalized by **Abraham Wald** in the framework of **Statistical Decision Theory**, and is often referred to as the **Pessimism Criterion** because it inherently assumes that the worst possible state of nature will materialize.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1: Decision Theory under Uncertainty)**
> The MaxiMin Criterion is one of the **four classical non-probabilistic criteria** for decision-making under uncertainty, alongside **MaxiMax, MiniMax Regret (Savage), and Laplace (Principle of Insufficient Reason)**. It is the most **risk-averse** of these criteria.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are planning a one-day outdoor picnic. You have three venue options:
- **Venue A (Beach)**: Fantastic if sunny (payoff = 100), terrible if it rains (payoff = 0).
- **Venue B (Mall)**: Good if sunny (payoff = 70), good if it rains (payoff = 60).
- **Venue C (Home)** Decent if sunny (payoff = 50), decent if it rains (payoff = 50).

You have **no weather forecast** — pure uncertainty. A **MaxiMin** decision-maker thinks:

> *"I cannot predict the weather. Let me assume the worst. If it rains at the Beach, I get 0. If it rains at the Mall, I get 60. If it rains at Home, I get 50. The best among these worst-case scenarios is the Mall (60). Hence, I choose Venue B."*
