# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: Define Quantitative Techniques. List any FOUR major classifications of QT with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Quantitative Techniques are mathematical and statistical methods used to convert qualitative managerial and engineering problems into numerical form so that optimal decisions can be derived logically and analytically.

**Four Classifications with Examples:**

| # | Classification | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mathematical Programming | Linear Programming for production planning |
| 2 | Probability and Statistics | Decision Tree analysis under risk |
| 3 | Forecasting Models | Time-Series Exponential Smoothing |
| 4 | Network and Project Models | PERT for project scheduling |

*(3 marks awarded: 1 for definition + 2 for classifications and examples)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q: Distinguish between Deterministic and Stochastic models. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Deterministic Model | Stochastic Model |
|---|---|---|
| Input Behavior | Fixed, known values | Random, probabilistic |
| Output | Single definite value | Probability distribution |
| Data Need | Exact parameters | Probability distributions |
| Example | Linear Programming | Queueing Theory / Markov Chain |

> Deterministic models assume complete knowledge of all input parameters, whereas stochastic models explicitly incorporate randomness and uncertainty into the mathematical formulation.

*(3 marks awarded: 2 for distinction table + 1 for examples)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** *(14 Marks)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 4]**

**Q: Explain in detail the classification of Quantitative Techniques used in engineering decision-making. Discuss the importance and applications of QT in any THREE engineering domains.**

#### Part (a) — Classification of Quantitative Techniques (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Introduce the conceptual foundation of QT classification.

Quantitative Techniques are classified along multiple axes depending on the nature of the problem, the mathematical foundation used, and the decision environment.

**Step 2:** Present the classification in a structured table.

| Classification Basis | Categories |
|---|---|
| Decision Environment | Certainty, Risk, Uncertainty, Conflict |
| Mathematical Nature | Linear, Non-Linear, Integer, Dynamic |
| Function | Descriptive, Predictive, Prescriptive |
| Data Type | Deterministic, Stochastic |

**Step 3:** Detail each classification axis.

- **By Decision Environment:**
  - *Certainty:* LP, Transportation, Assignment
  - *Risk:* Decision Trees, PERT
  - *Uncertainty:* Simulation, Fuzzy Logic
  - *Conflict:* Game Theory

- **By Function:**
  - *Descriptive:* Averages, Variance
  - *Predictive:* Regression, Time-Series
  - *Prescriptive:* LP, DP, Game Theory

*(7 marks awarded: 2 for table + 3 for elaboration + 2 for examples)*

---

#### Part (b) — Importance and Applications in Three Engineering Domains (7 Marks)

**Domain 1 — Civil Engineering:**

- *Application:* Construction project scheduling using **CPM/PERT**.
- *Importance:* Identifies the critical path, minimizes project delays, optimizes resource deployment across multi-stage construction projects such as bridges and high-rise buildings.

**Domain 2 — Mechanical Engineering:**

- *Application:* Production planning and inventory control using **Linear Programming and EOQ models**.
- *Importance:* Minimizes raw material holding cost while ensuring uninterrupted production flow in assembly-line manufacturing.

**Domain 3 — Electrical Engineering:**

- *Application:* Reliability engineering and queueing analysis of telecommunication networks.
- *Importance:* Models packet-switching delays, optimizes bandwidth allocation, and predicts congestion probabilities in service-provider networks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synthesis Statement:** Across all three domains, QT provides a common mathematical language that transforms intuition-based decisions into evidence-based optimal decisions.

*(7 marks awarded: 2 per domain × 3 domains + 1 for synthesis statement)*

---

### **Question B** *(Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 4]**

**Q: "Quantitative Techniques are indispensable tools for modern engineering managers." Critically evaluate this statement by classifying QT, discussing its importance, and demonstrating its application through a worked numerical example.**

#### Part (a) — Critical Evaluation and Classification (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Affirm the statement with reasoning.

Quantitative Techniques have become indispensable because modern engineering projects involve:
- Massive scale (large infrastructure, global supply chains)
- High capital investment (risk of wrong decisions is enormous)
- Multiple competing objectives (cost vs quality vs time)
- Significant uncertainty (market volatility, demand fluctuation)

**Step 2:** Provide a structured classification that supports the evaluation.

| Decision Need | Appropriate QT |
|---|---|
| Resource Optimization | Linear Programming |
| Demand Forecasting | Time-Series Analysis |
| Project Management | PERT / CPM |
| Inventory Control | EOQ Model |
| Competitive Strategy | Game Theory |
| Service System Design | Queueing Theory |

**Step 3:** Conclude the evaluation.

The statement is fully justified — QT converts vague managerial intuitions into precise, repeatable, and defensible decisions.

*(7 marks awarded: 2 for affirmation logic + 3 for classification table + 2 for conclusion)*

---

#### Part (b) — Worked Numerical Application Example (7 Marks)

**Problem:** A factory produces two products $X$ and $Y$. Product $X$ requires 2 hours on Machine $A$ and 1 hour on Machine $B$; Product $Y$ requires 1 hour on Machine $A$ and 3 hours on Machine $B$. Machine $A$ is available for 100 hours and Machine $B$ for 120 hours. Profit per unit is ₹50 for $X$ and ₹70 for $Y$. Formulate and classify the QT model.

**Step 1 — Decision Variables:**

Let $x_1$ = units of $X$, $x_2$ = units of $Y$.

**Step 2 — Objective Function:**

$$
\max Z \;=\; 50 x_1 + 70 x_2
$$

**Step 3 — Constraints:**

$$
2x_1 + x_2 \;\le\; 100 \quad \text{(Machine A)}
$$

$$
x_1 + 3x_2 \;\le\; 120 \quad \text{(Machine B)}
$$

$$
x_1, x_2 \;\ge\; 0 \quad \text{(Non-negativity)}
$$

**Step 4 — Classification:**

| Feature | Value |
|---|---|
| QT Family | Linear Programming |
| Solver | Simplex Method / Graphical |
| Decision Environment | Certainty |
| Function Type | Prescriptive / Optimization |

**Step 5 — Graphical Interpretation:**

Plot the two constraint lines, identify the feasible region, and evaluate $Z$ at corner points. The corner point yielding the highest $Z$ is the optimum.

*(7 marks awarded: 1 for variables + 2 for objective + 2 for constraints + 2 for classification and interpretation)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Examiner's Advisory**
> 1. **Sign Convention Omission:** Students forget to state whether the objective is **Maximize** or **Minimize**. Always state it explicitly — costs 1 mark if missing.
> 2. **Non-Negativity Constraint Omission:** The constraint $x_i \geq 0$ is mandatory in every LP formulation. Examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Confusing PERT with CPM:** PERT uses *three-time estimates* (probabilistic), while CPM uses *single-time estimates* (deterministic). Mixing them costs substantial marks.
> 4. **Skipping Classification:** When asked to "discuss applications," students often describe scenarios without naming the **specific QT family**. Always pair each application with its technique name.
> 5. **Wrong Formula Variants in EOQ:** Some students confuse EOQ with Economic Production Quantity (EPQ). Remember, EOQ assumes **instantaneous replenishment**; EPQ assumes **gradual production**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4**

- **Quantitative Techniques (QT)** = mathematical + statistical methods for decision-making.
- **Four Major Classifications:** Mathematical Programming, Probability & Statistics, Forecasting, Network & Project Models.
- **Three Functional Categories:** Descriptive, Predictive, Prescriptive.
- **Four Decision Environments:** Certainty, Risk, Uncertainty, Conflict.
- **Deterministic vs Stochastic:** Deterministic inputs are fixed; stochastic inputs involve probability distributions.
- **Linear Programming (LP):** Most fundamental optimization QT; canonical form uses $Z = c^T x$, subject to $Ax \leq b$, $x \geq 0$.
- **PERT Formula:** $t_e = (a + 4m + b) / 6$; Variance = $(b-a)^2 / 36$.
- **CPM Slack Formula:** $\text{Slack} = LS - ES = LF - EF$; zero slack identifies the critical path.
- **EOQ Formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$; valid for constant demand, instantaneous replenishment, no stockouts.
- **Game Theory Saddle Point:** Occurs when $\max_i \min_j a_{ij} = \min_j \max_i a_{ij}$.
- **Decision Tree EMV:** Multiply each branch payoff by its probability and sum across branches; choose the branch with the highest EMV (for maximization).
- **Queueing M/M/1:** Single-server, Poisson arrivals, exponential service; $L_q = \lambda^2 / [\mu(\mu - \lambda)]$.
- **Time-Series Moving Average:** Smooths fluctuations; weight equal to $1/n$ per past observation.
- **Engineering Applications (must memorize):**
  - Civil → CPM/PERT
  - Mechanical → EOQ, LP for production
  - Electrical → Reliability, Queueing for telecom
  - Computer Science → Simulation, Network optimization
- **Always state:** Maximize/Minimize, decision variables, all constraints including $x_i \geq 0$.
- **Numerical accuracy:** Carry at least 2 decimal places in all KTU numerical answers.
- **Diagrams win marks:** Always include a graphical or tabular representation — examiners reward visual clarity.
- **Mapping rule:** Identify decision environment → match to QT family → formulate model → solve → validate.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Measures in Queuing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET612 - QUANTITATIVE TECHNIQUES FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Performance Measures in Queuing Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Measures (Operating Characteristics)** of a queuing system are quantitative statistical metrics that describe the steady-state behavioral efficiency of a service facility. These measures quantify the *average* number of customers present, the *average* time spent, the *probability* of finding the system in various states, and the *utilization* of service resources.

For a generic queuing system represented by Kendall's notation **(a/b/c : d/e/f)**, the standard KTU-evaluated performance measures are:

- **L** — Expected (average) number of customers in the **entire system** (queue + service).
- **Lq** — Expected number of customers in the **queue only** (waiting, not being served).
- **W** — Expected **total time** a customer spends in the system (waiting + service).
- **Wq** — Expected **waiting time** in the queue (excluding service time).
- **ρ** — **Traffic intensity / Utilization factor** of the service facility.
- **Pn** — Steady-state **probability** that exactly *n* customers are in the system.
- **P0** — Steady-state **probability** that the system is **empty / idle**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The KSRTC Bus Station Counter, Trivandrum**
>
> Imagine the single reservation counter at the Central Bus Station. You, the customer, *arrive* randomly. The clerk *serves* you one at a time. Sometimes the queue is long, sometimes empty.
>
> - **L** counts *everyone* inside the station waiting plus the one at the counter — like the total crowd in the booking hall.
> - **Lq** counts only those *standing in line* — those waiting but not yet at the counter.
> - **W** is the total time from the moment you entered the hall to the moment you walked out with your ticket.
> - **Wq** is the time you stood in the queue *before* the clerk called you.
> - **ρ** is how *busy* the clerk is. If ρ = 0.8, the clerk is busy 80 % of the time and idle only 20 %.
> - **P0** is the probability that you walk in and find *no one* at the counter at all.
> - **Pn** is the probability of finding exactly *n* people (queue + service) when you enter.
>
> Performance Measures simply translate this *human experience* of waiting into *numbers* that engineers use to design optimal service facilities (bank counters, hospital OPDs, toll booths, telecom servers, manufacturing buffers).

## 1.3 Why Performance Measures Matter

| Engineering Application | Performance Measure Used |
|---|---|
| Telecom call center design | **W**, **Wq** (customer tolerance limit) |
| Hospital emergency room staffing | **Lq**, **P0** (patient queue length) |
| Manufacturing conveyor / machine cell | **ρ** (machine utilization) |
| Web server capacity planning | **W** (response time SLA) |
| Airport runway / toll plaza lane design | **c** choice via **Lq** cost balance |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Universal Relationships (Little's Law & Conservation)

The most powerful result in queuing theory is **Little's Law** (proved by John D. C. Little, 1961). It holds for *any* queuing system that has reached **steady state** (long-run equilibrium, not transient startup).

### 2.1.1 Little's Law for the System

$$L = \lambda \cdot W$$

- **λ** = effective arrival rate (customers per unit time)
- **W** = average time a customer spends in the system
- **L** = average number of customers in the system

### 2.1.2 Little's Law for the Queue Only

$$L_q = \lambda \cdot W_q$$

### 2.1.3 The Conservation / Additivity Identity

$$L = L_q + \rho$$

and equivalently

$$W = W_q + \frac{1}{\mu}$$

where **1/μ** is the mean service time per customer.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter?** Once you compute any TWO measures, the remaining measures are *automatically* determined. This is the single most common shortcut used in KTU valuation — examiners award full marks when a student uses Little's Law to skip a long derivation.

## 2.2 Notation and Symbols Standard

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| λ | Mean arrival rate | customers / hour |
| μ | Mean service rate per server | customers / hour |
| ρ | Traffic intensity = λ / (c μ) | dimensionless |
| c | Number of identical servers | count |
| L | Mean number in system | count |
| Lq | Mean number in queue | count |
| W | Mean time in system | hours |
| Wq | Mean waiting time in queue | hours |
| P0 | Probability of zero customers | probability |
| Pn | Probability of exactly n customers | probability |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Standard Birth–Death Queues (M/M/c family)

### 2.3.1 M / M / 1 — Single Server, Infinite Capacity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The M/M/1 model** assumes Poisson arrivals, exponential service, single server, FIFO discipline, infinite queue capacity, and unlimited population. Stability requires **ρ < 1**, i.e., **λ < μ**.

$$P_0 = 1 - \rho$$

$$P_n = (1 - \rho)\,\rho^n \quad \text{for } n \geq 0$$

$$L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho} = \frac{\lambda^2}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}$$

$$L = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}$$

$$W_q = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}$$

$$W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$$

### 2.3.2 M / M / 1 : (N) — Finite Capacity (N, FIFO)

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$$

$$P_0 = \frac{1 - \rho}{1 - \rho^{N+1}} \quad \text{if } \rho \neq 1$$

$$P_n = P_0 \, \rho^n = \frac{(1-\rho)\,\rho^n}{1-\rho^{N+1}}$$

$$L = \frac{\rho \bigl[1 - (N+1)\rho^N + N\rho^{N+1}\bigr]}{(1-\rho)(1-\rho^{N+1})}$$

$$L_q = L - (1 - P_0)$$

$$W = \frac{L}{\lambda(1 - P_N)}$$

> **NOTE:** λ in the finite-capacity case is the *arrival rate when the system is not full*; the *effective* arrival rate is $\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \lambda(1 - P_N)$.

### 2.3.3 M / M / c — Multiple Servers (c ≥ 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The M/M/c model** has Poisson arrivals, exponential service, c parallel identical servers, common FIFO queue. Per-server utilization is **ρ = λ / (c μ)**, with **ρ < 1** required.

$$P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} \;+\; \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!\,(1-\rho)} \right]^{-1}$$

$$P_n = \begin{cases} \dfrac{(c\rho)^n}{n!}\,P_0, & 0 \leq n \leq c \\[6pt] \dfrac{c^c\,\rho^n}{c!}\,P_0, & n \geq c \end{cases}$$

$$L_q = P_0 \, \frac{(c\rho)^c\,\rho}{c!\,(1-\rho)^2}$$

$$W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda} \qquad L = L_q + \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \qquad W = W_q + \frac{1}{\mu}$$

### 2.3.4 M / M / ∞ — Infinite Servers (Self-Service / Pure Delay)

$$P_n = \frac{e^{-\lambda/\mu}}{n!}\left(\frac{\lambda}{\mu}\right)^n$$

$$L = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \qquad L_q = 0 \qquad W = \frac{1}{\mu} \qquad W_q = 0$$

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why this metric is critical |
|---|---|
| **Telecom (Erlang B / C)** | Optimal trunk line count |
| **Manufacturing JIT cells** | Buffer size before blocking occurs |
| **Hospital ER triage** | Probability of finding empty bed (P0) |
| **Cloud / Web servers** | p95 latency budget = W |
| **Airport runways** | Optimal runway capacity c |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Little's Law (KTU High-Yield Concept)

### 3.1.1 Intuition

Imagine observing the system for a long time **T**. Let **N(T)** be the total cumulative number of customer-arrivals in (0, T] and **Ti** be the *time* the *i*-th customer spends inside the system. The *area under the system-occupancy curve* equals the sum of all dwell times.

The average number of customers in the system over the period **T** is:

$$\bar{L}(T) = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} N(t)\, dt$$

where **N(t)** is the instantaneous number of customers at time **t**.

The integral of N(t) over (0, T] is exactly the cumulative **total time spent in the system** by *all* customers who arrived in (0, T]:

$$\int_{0}^{T} N(t)\, dt = \sum_{i=1}^{N(T)} T_i$$

Hence:

$$\bar{L}(T) = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{i=1}^{N(T)} T_i$$

Divide and multiply by **N(T)**:

$$\bar{L}(T) = \frac{N(T)}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{N(T)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(T)} T_i$$

In the limit **T → ∞**:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \bar{L}(T) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{N(T)}{T} \cdot \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{N(T)}\sum_{i=1}^{N(T)} T_i$$

By definition:

$$\lambda = \lim_{T \to \infty}\frac{N(T)}{T}, \qquad W = \lim_{T \to \infty}\frac{1}{N(T)}\sum_{i=1}^{N(T)} T_i$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,L = \lambda W\,}$$

The proof for **Lq = λ Wq** is identical — restrict the integral to only those customers *waiting in queue*.

## 3.2 Derivation of L for M/M/1 from Birth–Death Balance

### 3.2.1 Steady-State Difference Equations

Let **Pn** be the steady-state probability of *n* customers. Birth rate **λn = λ**, death rate **μn = μ** for n ≥ 1, **μ0 = 0**. Steady-state balance:

$$(\lambda + \mu)\,P_n = \lambda\,P_{n-1} + \mu\,P_{n+1}, \quad n \geq 1$$

Rearrange:

$$P_{n+1} - P_n = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}(P_n - P_{n-1})$$

### 3.2.2 Geometric Recursion

This is a *geometric* recursion. Let **ρ = λ/μ**. Define **Δn = Pn − Pn−1**:

$$\Delta_{n+1} = \rho\, \Delta_n \implies \Delta_n = \rho^{n-1}\Delta_1 = \rho^{n-1}(P_1 - P_0)$$

Telescoping:

$$P_n - P_0 = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \Delta_k = (P_1 - P_0)\sum_{k=1}^{n} \rho^{k-1} = (P_1 - P_0)\,\frac{1 - \rho^n}{1-\rho}$$

Apply boundary balance at n = 0:

$$\lambda P_0 = \mu P_1 \implies P_1 = \rho P_0 \implies P_1 - P_0 = -(1-\rho)P_0$$

Therefore:

$$P_n = P_0 - (1-\rho)P_0 \cdot \frac{1-\rho^n}{1-\rho} = P_0\bigl[1 - (1-\rho^n)\bigr] = P_0\,\rho^n$$

### 3.2.3 Normalization

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_n = 1 \implies P_0 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^n = P_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1-\rho} = 1$$

So:

$$P_0 = 1 - \rho$$

and the **probability mass function** is:

$$P_n = (1-\rho)\,\rho^n$$

### 3.2.4 Expected Number in System

$$L = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n P_n = (1-\rho)\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n\,\rho^n$$

Use the geometric series identity $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n x^n = \dfrac{x}{(1-x)^2}$ for $\vert x \vert < 1$:

$$L = (1-\rho)\,\frac{\rho}{(1-\rho)^2} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}$$

Substitute $\rho = \lambda/\mu$:

$$L = \frac{\lambda/\mu}{1 - \lambda/\mu} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}$$

### 3.2.5 Expected Number in Queue

$$L_q = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(n-1)\,P_n = L - (1 - P_0) = L - \rho = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} - \rho = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$$

### 3.2.6 Time Measures via Little's Law

$$W = \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$$

$$W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda} = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — M/M/1 (KTU-Style Full Solution)

**Problem.** Cars arrive at a toll booth at λ = 30 cars/hour. Single attendant serves at μ = 40 cars/hour (both Poisson / exponential). Compute P0, L, Lq, W, Wq and the probability that the queue exceeds 5 cars.

### Step 1 — Compute Traffic Intensity

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75$$

> Stability check: ρ < 1 ✓

### Step 2 — Idle Probability

$$P_0 = 1 - \rho = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25$$

> There is a **25 %** chance the booth is empty at any instant.

### Step 3 — Mean Number in System

$$L = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} = \frac{0.75}{0.25} = 3 \text{ cars}$$

### Step 4 — Mean Number in Queue

$$L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho} = \frac{0.5625}{0.25} = 2.25 \text{ cars}$$

### Step 5 — Mean Time in System (Little's Law)

$$W = \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{3}{30} = 0.1 \text{ hours} = 6 \text{ minutes}$$

### Step 6 — Mean Waiting Time in Queue

$$W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda} = \frac{2.25}{30} = 0.075 \text{ hours} = 4.5 \text{ minutes}$$

> Sanity check: $W = W_q + 1/\mu = 0.075 + 0.025 = 0.1$ ✓

### Step 7 — Probability of Queue Length Exceeding 5

$$P(N > 5) = \sum_{n=6}^{\infty} P_n = \rho^6 = (0.75)^6$$

Compute:

$$(0.75)^6 = 0.1780$$

> There is a **17.8 %** chance of having more than 5 cars (queue + service) in the system.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Birth–Death Variants

| Model | λn | μn | Stability | P0 | Lq |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| M/M/1 | λ | μ (n≥1) | ρ < 1 | 1−ρ | ρ²/(1−ρ) |
| M/M/1 : (N) | λ (n < N), 0 (n ≥ N) | μ (n ≥ 1) | any ρ | (1−ρ)/(1−ρ^(N+1)) | L − (1−P0) |
| M/M/c | λ | nμ (1 ≤ n < c), cμ (n ≥ c) | ρ < 1, ρ = λ/(cμ) | inverse sum | P0 (cρ)^c ρ / [c!(1−ρ)²] |
| M/M/∞ | λ | nμ | always | — | 0 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

    q1 -- NO --> model1[USE G OR GK MODEL EMPIRICAL]:::branchNode
    q1 -- YES --> q2{SERVICE TIME EXPONENTIAL}:::decision
    q2 -- NO --> model2[USE M G 1 OR M G c ERLANG FAMILY]:::branchNode

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — M/M/1 Birth–Death Chain

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> s0
    s0: STATE 0 EMPTY
    s1: STATE 1 ONE CUSTOMER
    s2: STATE 2 TWO CUSTOMERS
    s3: STATE 3 THREE CUSTOMERS
    sN: STATE N N CUSTOMERS

    s0 --> s1: ARRIVAL LAMBDA
    s1 --> s0: SERVICE MU
    s1 --> s2: ARRIVAL LAMBDA
    s2 --> s1: SERVICE MU
    s2 --> s3: ARRIVAL LAMBDA
    s3 --> s2: SERVICE MU
    s3 --> sN: ARRIVAL LAMBDA
    sN --> s3: SERVICE MU
```

> **Reading:** In state *n*, an arrival sends the system to *n+1* at rate **λ**, and a service completion sends it to *n−1* at rate **μ**. The steady-state balance between **λ Pn** and **μ Pn+1** yields the geometric distribution **Pn = (1−ρ) ρⁿ**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** State and prove Little's Law for a queuing system. Why is it considered a *general* result?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement.** In any queuing system that has reached steady state, the long-run average number of customers **L** in the system equals the product of the long-run average effective arrival rate **λ** and the long-run average time **W** spent by a customer in the system: **L = λ W**. An analogous relation **Lq = λ Wq** holds for the queue alone.

**Proof Sketch.**
Let **T** be a long observation window, **N(T)** the number of arrivals, and **Ti** the *i*-th customer's dwell time.

$$L \approx \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T N(t)\,dt = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{i=1}^{N(T)} T_i = \underbrace{\frac{N(T)}{T}}_{\to\,\lambda} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{N(T)}\sum_{i=1}^{N(T)} T_i}_{\to\,W}$$

Taking **T → ∞** yields **L = λ W**.

**Generality.** Little's Law depends only on *conservation of flow* and does not require Markovian (Poisson/exponential) assumptions. It applies to M/M/1, M/M/c, G/G/1, finite-capacity, finite-population, multi-server networks, and even non-queuing stock-flow systems. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define the **traffic intensity ρ** for an M/M/1 system and explain what happens as **ρ → 1⁻**.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition.** For an M/M/1 system: $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ = (mean service time) × (arrival rate). It is also called the **server utilization factor**, the *probability* the server is busy.

**Behaviour as ρ → 1⁻.**

$$L = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} \to \infty, \qquad W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} \to \infty, \qquad L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho} \to \infty$$

> Physically, the server is busy **almost always**, the queue grows without bound, and customer waiting time diverges. This is the **ergodic / stability boundary**: any further increase in **λ** (or decrease in **μ**) makes the system **non-ergodic**, and steady-state probabilities do not exist. Practically, designers keep **ρ ≤ 0.8–0.85** for acceptable service. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

> Customers arrive at a single-server railway reservation counter at the rate of **λ = 10 per hour** according to a Poisson process. The service time is **exponential with mean 6 minutes**.
>
> **(a)** Identify the queuing model and compute **P0, L, Lq, W, Wq** and the probability that an arriving customer has to wait. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** If the management installs **one additional identical server** (M/M/2 with same arrival and service rates), recompute **P0, Lq, Wq** and comment on whether the upgrade is justified. (7 marks)

#### Solution A(a) — M/M/1

**Step 1 — Standardize units.**
Service rate μ = 60 / 6 = **10 per hour**. Arrival rate λ = **10 per hour**.

**Step 2 — Compute ρ.**

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{10}{10} = 1.0$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** A common error here is to *compute* the remaining metrics using M/M/1 formulas when ρ = 1. The system is **at the stability boundary** — *L, Lq, W, Wq → ∞*. The student must explicitly state that steady-state **does not exist** and recommend that the service rate be increased (μ > 10/hr) or a second server added (Part b).

**Diagnosis.** With ρ = 1.0, the system is critically loaded; mean measures are not finite in steady state. Therefore, no finite values for L, Lq, W, Wq can be reported under M/M/1.

**Step 3 — Probability an arriving customer must wait.**

In M/M/1, the probability an arriving customer must wait = probability server is busy = ρ.

$$P(\text{wait}) = \rho = 1.0$$

> **Conclusion (a):** The single-server system is **unstable / marginally stable**. Management **must** add capacity — taken up in part (b). **(7 marks)**

#### Solution A(b) — M/M/2

**Step 1 — Parameters.** λ = 10/hr, μ = 10/hr, c = 2.

**Step 2 — Per-server utilization.**

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c\mu} = \frac{10}{2 \times 10} = 0.5$$

Stability: ρ < 1 ✓.

**Step 3 — Compute P0.**

$$\frac{c\rho}{1} = 2 \times 0.5 = 1$$

The summation:

$$P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1}\frac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} + \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!\,(1-\rho)} \right]^{-1}$$

Compute each term:

- n=0: $(c\rho)^0/0! = 1$
- n=1: $(c\rho)^1/1! = 1$
- c term: $(c\rho)^c / [c!(1-\rho)] = 1^2 / [2! \times 0.5] = 1/1 = 1$

Sum = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3.

$$P_0 = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$

**Step 4 — Lq.**

$$L_q = P_0 \, \frac{(c\rho)^c\, \rho}{c!\,(1-\rho)^2} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1 \cdot 0.5}{2 \cdot (0.5)^2}$$

$$L_q = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{0.5}{2 \cdot 0.25} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{0.5}{0.5} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$

**Step 5 — Wq (Little's Law).**

$$W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda} = \frac{0.3333}{10} = 0.03333 \text{ hr} = 2 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 6 — P(wait) for M/M/c.**

In M/M/c, P(wait) = Erlang-C = probability all servers are busy:

$$P(\text{wait}) = \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!\,(1-\rho)} \cdot P_0 = 1 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$

**Conclusion (b):** With 2 servers, **Wq drops from ∞ to just 2 minutes**, and P(wait) drops from 1.0 to 0.333. The upgrade is **strongly justified**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternate Choice]**

> A small BPO (Business Process Outsourcing) unit receives customer calls at **λ = 24 per hour** (Poisson). It has a single IVR system that handles each call in **exponentially distributed time with mean 2 minutes**. A buffer can hold at most **N = 4 calls** (in queue + service). Calls that arrive when the buffer is full are *lost*.
>
> **(a)** Identify the model. Compute the **steady-state probabilities P0, P1, P2, P3, P4**, and the **effective arrival rate λeff** (accounting for lost calls). (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Compute **L, Lq, W, Wq** for this finite-capacity system. (7 marks)

#### Solution B(a) — M/M/1 : (N=4)

**Step 1 — Parameter check.**

$$\lambda = 24/\text{hr}, \qquad \mu = 60/2 = 30/\text{hr}, \qquad \rho = \lambda/\mu = 24/30 = 0.8$$

**Step 2 — Model identification.** This is **M/M/1 : (N = 4) / FIFO / ∞ population** — single exponential server, finite capacity 4 (system capacity, not just queue), Poisson arrivals.

**Step 3 — Normalizing constant P0.**

$$P_0 = \frac{1-\rho}{1-\rho^{N+1}} = \frac{1 - 0.8}{1 - (0.8)^5}$$

Compute $(0.8)^5$:

$$0.8^2 = 0.64,\quad 0.8^3 = 0.512,\quad 0.8^4 = 0.4096,\quad 0.8^5 = 0.32768$$

Therefore:

$$P_0 = \frac{0.20}{1 - 0.32768} = \frac{0.20}{0.67232} \approx 0.2975$$

**Step 4 — State probabilities Pn = P0 ρⁿ.**

- $P_1 = 0.2975 \times 0.8 = 0.2380$
- $P_2 = 0.2975 \times 0.64 = 0.1904$
- $P_3 = 0.2975 \times 0.512 = 0.1523$
- $P_4 = 0.2975 \times 0.4096 = 0.1218$

> **Sanity check:** Sum = 0.2975 + 0.2380 + 0.1904 + 0.1523 + 0.1218 = **1.0000** ✓.

**Step 5 — Effective arrival rate (lost-call formula).**

$$\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \lambda(1 - P_N) = 24(1 - P_4) = 24(1 - 0.1218) = 24 \times 0.8782 \approx 21.08 \text{ calls/hr}$$

> About **2.92 calls/hr** are lost (blocked) due to full buffer. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution B(b) — Compute L, Lq, W, Wq

**Step 1 — L (expected number in system).**

$$L = \sum_{n=0}^{4} n P_n = 1(0.2380) + 2(0.1904) + 3(0.1523) + 4(0.1218)$$

$$L = 0.2380 + 0.3808 + 0.4569 + 0.4872 = 1.5629 \text{ calls}$$

**Step 2 — Lq (expected number in queue).**

$$L_q = L - (1 - P_0) = 1.5629 - (1 - 0.2975) = 1.5629 - 0.7025 = 0.8604 \text{ calls}$$

> **Alternative formula:** $L_q = \sum_{n=1}^{4}(n-1)P_n = 0(0.238) + 1(0.1904) + 2(0.1523) + 3(0.1218) = 0.8604$ ✓

**Step 3 — W (Little's Law using λeff).**

$$W = \frac{L}{\lambda_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{1.5629}{21.08} \approx 0.07414 \text{ hr} \approx 4.45 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4 — Wq.**

$$W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{0.8604}{21.08} \approx 0.04082 \text{ hr} \approx 2.45 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 5 — Verification via conservation.**

$$W = W_q + \frac{1}{\mu} = 0.04082 + 0.03333 = 0.07415 \text{ hr} \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always use λeff = λ(1 − P_N)** in Little's Law for finite-capacity systems — using raw λ inflates W and Wq incorrectly.
> 2. **Do NOT** use the infinite-capacity M/M/1 formula $L = \rho/(1-\rho)$ here. The finite-capacity denominator $1 - \rho^{N+1}$ would be missed.
> 3. State-probability summation must be **normalised**; off-by-one errors in Pn indexing (n = 0 vs n = 1) cost full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Performance Measures in Queuing Systems — At a Glance**

- **Little's Law (universal):** **L = λ W**, **Lq = λ Wq**. Valid for *any* steady-state queuing model — no distributional assumptions needed.
- **Conservation identity:** **L = Lq + ρ** and **W = Wq + 1/μ**.
- **M/M/1 formulas:** **P0 = 1 − ρ**, **Pn = (1−ρ)ρⁿ**, **L = ρ/(1−ρ)**, **Lq = ρ²/(1−ρ)**, **W = 1/(μ−λ)**, **Wq = ρ/(μ−λ)**.
- **Stability rule:** M/M/1 requires **ρ = λ/μ < 1**; M/M/c requires **ρ = λ/(cμ) < 1**.
- **M/M/c formula:** $P_0 = \bigl[\sum_{n=0}^{c-1}(c\rho)^n/n! \;+\; (c\rho)^c/[c!\,(1-\rho)]\bigr]^{-1}$; $L_q = P_0 (c\rho)^c \rho/[c!\,(1-\rho)^2]$.
- **M/M/1 : (N) finite capacity:** $P_0 = (1-\rho)/(1-\rho^{N+1})$, **effective arrival** $\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \lambda(1 - P_N)$, always used in Little's Law.
- **M/M/∞:** Pure delay, **Lq = 0**, **Wq = 0**, **L = λ/μ**, **W = 1/μ**, **Pn = Poisson distribution**.
- **Design heuristic:** Keep **ρ ≤ 0.8–0.85** for acceptable customer service in real-world single-server systems; for **M/M/c**, target **ρ ≤ 0.7** per server.
- **Probability of waiting (M/M/1):** = ρ = 1 − P0. **Probability all c servers busy (M/M/c):** = Erlang-C = $\frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!(1-\rho)} P_0$.
- **Pitfall avoidance:** Verify ρ < 1 *before* computing L; in finite-capacity use λeff, not λ; remember 1/μ has unit of *time*, not rate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Single-Server Exponential Arrival and Service Times (M/M/1 Queue)

### Formal Academic Definition
The **M/M/1 Queuing System** is the most fundamental birth-death based stochastic service model in Queueing Theory, where:
- The **first M** denotes **Markovian (Poisson) Arrivals** — inter-arrival times are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) random variables following an **Exponential distribution** with parameter **λ** (mean arrival rate).
- The **second M** denotes **Markovian Service Times** — service durations are i.i.d. exponential random variables with parameter **μ** (mean service rate).
- The **1** denotes a **Single Server** providing service on a **First-Come-First-Served (FCFS)** basis from a single waiting line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kendall's Notation:** M/M/1/K/N/D where K = system capacity, N = population size, D = service discipline. The base M/M/1 assumes infinite capacity and infinite population.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The KTU Cafeteria Counter 🍽️**
> Imagine a single counter at the KTU main canteen. Students arrive randomly (Poisson process — no fixed schedule; sometimes 5 in a minute, sometimes none). Each student's billing + payment time is random (Exponential — some are quick with UPI, some fumble with cash). There is one server. This entire setup is an **M/M/1 queue**. The question we ask is: *On average, how long does a student wait? How many are in line? Is the server overburdened?*

### Key System Metrics (Standard KTU Symbols)

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| **λ** | Mean arrival rate (customers per unit time) | customers/hour |
| **μ** | Mean service rate (customers per unit time) | customers/hour |
| **ρ = λ/μ** | Traffic intensity / Server utilization factor | dimensionless |
| **L** | Expected number of customers in the system | customers |
| **Lq** | Expected number in the queue (excluding the one in service) | customers |
| **W** | Expected time a customer spends in the system | time units |
| **Wq** | Expected waiting time in queue | time units |
| **Pn** | Steady-state probability of exactly n customers in system | dimensionless |
| **s** | Number of servers | integer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Condition:** For a steady-state solution to exist, we **must** have **ρ = λ/μ < 1**. If ρ ≥ 1, the queue grows without bound and explodes.

---

## 1.2 Multi-Server Problems (M/M/c Model)

### Formal Definition
The **M/M/c Queuing System** is a generalization of M/M/1 with **c identical parallel servers** serving from a **single common queue** on an FCFS basis. Each server has an independent exponential service rate **μ**, and the combined service capacity becomes **cμ**.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The KTU Central Library Issue Counter 📚**
> At the KTU central library, there are **3 issue counters** (c = 3). Students form **one single queue** in front of the counters (not three separate lines). Whichever counter becomes free first serves the next student. Each counter serves at an exponential rate μ. The combined service rate of the system is 3μ. If the arrival rate λ is high, three counters dramatically reduce waiting time compared to a single counter.

---

## 1.3 Monte Carlo Simulation

### Formal Definition
**Monte Carlo Simulation** is a computational stochastic technique that uses **repeated random sampling** from known probability distributions to obtain numerical estimates for deterministic problems that are analytically intractable or too complex to solve via closed-form mathematics. It is widely used for risk analysis, optimization, and integration.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Estimating π with Darts 🎯**
> To estimate π, randomly throw darts at a square that contains an inscribed circle. The ratio of darts landing inside the circle to total darts, multiplied by 4, approximates π. Each dart throw is a *random sample*. Thousands of throws give a converging estimate. This is Monte Carlo simulation in its purest form!

---

## 1.4 Queuing Simulation Model

### Formal Definition
A **Queuing Simulation Model** is a discrete-event stochastic simulation of a real queueing system where, instead of using closed-form M/M/1 or M/M/c formulas, we **manually generate arrival times and service times** using random numbers and event-by-event clock advances to compute performance metrics.

---

## 1.5 Generation of Random Numbers

### Formal Definition
**Random Number Generation (RNG)** is the process of producing a sequence of numbers that pass standard tests for randomness (uniformity, independence). In simulation, we need:
- **Uniform (0,1) random numbers** — generated via mathematical algorithms.
- **Non-uniform random variates** — generated by transforming uniform random numbers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A computer-generated sequence is **pseudo-random** because it is produced by a deterministic algorithm, but it passes all statistical tests for randomness.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 M/M/1 Queue — Birth-Death Process Foundation

A **Birth-Death Process** is a continuous-time Markov chain where:
- **Birth** = arrival of a customer (state increases by 1), governed by λn.
- **Death** = departure of a customer (state decreases by 1), governed by μn.

For **M/M/1**, the transition rates are constant:
- λn = λ for all n ≥ 0 (arrival rate independent of n)
- μn = μ for n ≥ 1 (service rate independent of n)

### Derivation of Steady-State Probabilities

Let **Pn** = steady-state probability that there are exactly **n** customers in the system.

Using the **balance equations** (rate into state n = rate out of state n):

$$\text{For } n = 0: \quad \lambda P_0 = \mu P_1$$

$$\text{For } n \geq 1: \quad \lambda P_{n-1} + \mu P_{n+1} = (\lambda + \mu) P_n$$

The general solution yields:

$$P_n = (1 - \rho) \, \rho^n \quad \text{for } n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

where **ρ = λ/μ**.

The probability of an empty system:
$$P_0 = 1 - \rho$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Distribution:** The number of customers in an M/M/1 system follows a geometric distribution with parameter (1 − ρ).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — M/M/1 Queue

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| **Server Utilization** | **ρ = λ/μ** | Fraction of time server is busy |
| **P0 (Idle Probability)** | **P0 = 1 − ρ** | Probability no customer in system |
| **Pn** | **(1 − ρ) ρⁿ** | Probability of n customers |
| **L** (Avg in System) | **ρ / (1 − ρ)** | Includes customer in service |
| **Lq** (Avg in Queue) | **ρ² / (1 − ρ)** | Excludes customer in service |
| **W** (Avg Time in System) | **1 / (μ − λ)** | Total sojourn time |
| **Wq** (Avg Waiting Time) | **λ / [μ(μ − λ)]** | Pure waiting time |
| **Probability of Waiting (n ≥ 2)** | **ρ** | Probability that an arrival must wait |

> [!NOTE]
> **Little's Law Verification:** L = λW and Lq = λWq hold for ALL queueing systems (not just M/M/1). This is a powerful sanity check!

---

## 2.3 M/M/c Queue — Theoretical Framework

For the **M/M/c** system with **c identical servers**:
- Combined service capacity when all servers are busy: **cμ**
- Traffic intensity: **ρ = λ/(cμ)** (note: here ρ is the **per-server utilization**, total system utilization = cρ)

### The Erlang-C Formula (Probability of Waiting)

The probability that an arriving customer must **wait** (because all c servers are busy):

$$P(\text{wait}) = P_w = \frac{\frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!}}{\left(1 - \rho\right) \sum_{k=0}^{c-1} \frac{(c\rho)^k}{k!} + \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Erlang's C Formula** is a critical KTU high-yield formula. It is also called **P(Delay)** or **P(Q > 0)** — the probability of queuing.

### KTU Formula Sheet — M/M/c Queue

| Quantity | Formula |
|----------|---------|
| **Server Utilization** | **ρ = λ/(cμ)** |
| **P0 (Idle Probability)** | $$ P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} + \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!} \cdot \frac{1}{1-\rho} \right]^{-1} $$ |
| **Probability of Waiting (Pw)** | **Erlang-C formula above** |
| **Lq** | $$ L_q = P_w \cdot \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} $$ |
| **Wq** | **Wq = Lq / λ** |
| **W** | **W = Wq + 1/μ** |
| **L** | **L = Lq + λ/μ** |

---

## 2.4 Monte Carlo Simulation — Methodology

### The Five-Step Procedure (KTU Standard)

1. **Define the Problem** — Identify the unknown quantity to estimate.
2. **Construct the Mathematical/Logical Model** — Define input distributions.
3. **Generate Random Inputs** — Use RNG for each input variable.
4. **Compute Outputs** — Run the model for each random input realization.
5. **Aggregate Results** — Average outputs over many replications; compute confidence intervals.

### Application Areas
- Estimating area under curves (numerical integration)
- Queueing and inventory analysis
- Financial risk modeling (VaR — Value at Risk)
- Reliability engineering
- Physics (particle transport, statistical mechanics)

---

## 2.5 Queuing Simulation Model — Event-Based Approach

In manual simulation, we advance the clock event by event:

| Event Type | Trigger | Action |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| **Arrival Event** | A new customer enters | If server idle → start service; else → join queue |
| **Departure Event** | A customer finishes service | If queue nonempty → start next service; else → server idle |

**Random Variate Generation from Uniform (0,1) R:**

- **Inter-arrival time** (Exponential mean = 1/λ):
$$A_i = -\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln(R_i)$$

- **Service time** (Exponential mean = 1/μ):
$$S_i = -\frac{1}{\mu} \ln(R_i)$$

- **Discrete arrival rate for n arrivals per hour:** Use cumulative probability table to map R → arrival count.

---

## 2.6 Random Number Generation Methods

### 2.6.1 Mid-Square Method (Von Neumann, 1946)
Given an n-digit seed x₀:
1. Square x₀ to get an integer (possibly up to 2n digits).
2. Extract the middle n digits → next random number.
3. Repeat.

### 2.6.2 Multiplicative Congruential Method
$$x_{n+1} = (a \cdot x_n) \mod m$$
$$R_{n+1} = x_{n+1} / m$$

### 2.6.3 Mixed (Linear) Congruential Method
$$x_{n+1} = (a \cdot x_n + c) \mod m$$
$$R_{n+1} = x_{n+1} / m$$

### 2.6.4 Additive Congruential Method
$$x_{n+1} = (x_{n-k} + x_{n-j}) \mod m \quad \text{with } k < j$$

### Properties a Good RNG Must Satisfy
- **Uniformity** — each number in [0, m−1] occurs with equal frequency.
- **Independence** — no correlation between successive numbers.
- **Efficiency** — fast computation.
- **Reproducibility** — same seed → same sequence (crucial for debugging).
- **Long Period** — cycle length before repetition should be large.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tests for Randomness** (KTU must-know):
> 1. **Chi-Square Test** for Uniformity
> 2. **Kolmogorov-Smirnov Test** for Distribution
> 3. **Runs Test (Up/Down)** for Independence
> 4. **Autocorrelation Test** for Serial Independence

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Steady-State Probabilities for M/M/1

### Setup of Balance Equations

Consider the **birth-death process** for M/M/1. In steady-state, for every state **n**, the rate of flow **into** state n must equal the rate of flow **out of** state n.

**Transition rates out of state n:**
- Birth (arrival): rate = λ
- Death (departure, valid only if n ≥ 1): rate = μ
- **Total exit rate = λ + μ** (for n ≥ 1) and **λ** for n = 0.

**Transition rates into state n:**
- From state n − 1 (a birth occurs): rate = λ · Pₙ₋₁
- From state n + 1 (a death occurs): rate = μ · Pₙ₊₁

### Writing the Equations

For **n = 0** (no death possible since system empty):

$$\lambda P_1 = \lambda P_0 \implies P_1 = \rho P_0$$

For **n = 1, 2, 3, ...** general balance:

$$(\lambda + \mu) P_n = \lambda P_{n-1} + \mu P_{n+1}$$

### Recursive Solution

Rearranging for Pₙ₊₁:

$$\mu P_{n+1} = (\lambda + \mu) P_n - \lambda P_{n-1} = \lambda P_n + \mu P_n - \lambda P_{n-1}$$

$$P_{n+1} - P_n = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}(P_n - P_{n-1}) = \rho(P_n - P_{n-1})$$

Define **Δn = Pₙ − Pₙ₋₁**. Then:

$$\Delta_{n+1} = \rho \cdot \Delta_n$$

By telescoping from n = 1:

$$\Delta_n = \rho^{n-1} \Delta_1 = \rho^{n-1} \rho P_0 = \rho^n P_0$$

Thus:

$$P_n = P_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{n} \Delta_k = P_0 + P_0 \sum_{k=1}^{n} \rho^k = P_0 \left(1 + \rho \cdot \frac{1 - \rho^n}{1 - \rho}\right) \text{ (geometric series)}$$

For **n ≥ 1**:

$$P_n = P_0 \cdot \frac{\rho - \rho^{n+1}}{1 - \rho} = (1-\rho) \cdot \frac{\rho - \rho^{n+1}}{1-\rho} \cdot P_0 / P_0$$

Wait — let me derive this more cleanly. Using **P₀ = 1 − ρ**:

$$P_n = (1 - \rho) \rho^n \quad \text{for } n \geq 0$$

**Normalization check:** Σ Pn = 1 → (1−ρ) Σ ρⁿ = (1−ρ) · 1/(1−ρ) = 1 ✓

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: M/M/1 Performance Metrics

### Derivation of L (Expected Number in System)

By definition:

$$L = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \cdot P_n = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n (1-\rho) \rho^n$$

$$L = (1-\rho) \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \rho^n = (1-\rho) \cdot \frac{\rho}{(1-\rho)^2} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}$$

using the identity Σ n·xⁿ = x/(1−x)² for |x| < 1.

Substituting ρ = λ/μ:

$$\boxed{L = \frac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}}$$

### Derivation of Lq (Expected Number in Queue)

$$L_q = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (n-1) P_n = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n P_n - \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} P_n$$

$$L_q = L - (1 - P_0) = L - \rho = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} - \rho = \frac{\rho - \rho(1-\rho)}{1-\rho} = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$$

### Derivation of W and Wq via Little's Law

**Little's Law:** L = λW ⇒ W = L/λ

$$W = \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{\rho}{\lambda(1-\rho)} = \frac{\lambda/\mu}{\lambda(1-\lambda/\mu)} = \frac{1/\mu}{1-\lambda/\mu} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$$

$$W_q = W - \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{\mu-\lambda} - \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu-\lambda)}$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Erlang-C Formula

For M/M/c, we first find **P₀** by solving Σ Pn = 1:

$$1 = P_0 \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{\lambda^n}{\mu^n n!} + \sum_{n=c}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^n}{\mu^n c! c^{n-c}} \right]$$

$$1 = P_0 \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} + \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!} \sum_{n=c}^{\infty} \rho^{n-c} \right]$$

$$1 = P_0 \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} + \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!} \cdot \frac{1}{1-\rho} \right]$$

Thus:

$$P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} + \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!(1-\rho)} \right]^{-1}$$

### Probability of Waiting

An arriving customer must wait if all c servers are busy, i.e., system is in state n ≥ c. We can show:

$$P_w = \frac{\lambda^c}{\mu^c c! (1-\rho)} \cdot P_0$$

Substituting P₀:

$$\boxed{P_w = \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!} \cdot \frac{1}{(1-\rho)\sum_{k=0}^{c-1}\frac{(c\rho)^k}{k!} + \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!}}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: M/M/1 Numerical Computation

**Problem:** A bank's single teller serves customers at rate μ = 50/hr. Customers arrive at rate λ = 30/hr. Find L, Lq, W, Wq, P0, and P(busy).

### Step 1: Compute ρ

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.6$$

Since ρ = 0.6 < 1, the system is **stable**.

### Step 2: Compute P0

$$P_0 = 1 - \rho = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4$$

### Step 3: Compute L

$$L = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} = \frac{0.6}{0.4} = 1.5 \text{ customers}$$

### Step 4: Compute Lq

$$L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho} = \frac{0.36}{0.4} = 0.9 \text{ customers}$$

### Step 5: Compute W (in hours)

$$W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{50 - 30} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ hr} = 3 \text{ minutes}$$

### Step 6: Compute Wq

$$W_q = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu-\lambda)} = \frac{30}{50 \times 20} = 0.03 \text{ hr} = 1.8 \text{ minutes}$$

**Verification via Little's Law:**

$$L = \lambda W = 30 \times 0.05 = 1.5 \checkmark$$
$$L_q = \lambda W_q = 30 \times 0.03 = 0.9 \checkmark$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Random Number Generation (Mid-Square Method)

**Problem:** Generate 4 random numbers using the Mid-Square Method with 4-digit seed x₀ = 2187.

### Iteration 1
- Square: (2187)² = 4782969
- Pad to 8 digits: 04782969
- Extract middle 4 digits: **7829**
- Random number R₁ = 0.7829

### Iteration 2
- Square: (7829)² = 61293241
- Pad to 8 digits: 61293241
- Extract middle 4 digits: **2932**
- Random number R₂ = 0.2932

### Iteration 3
- Square: (2932)² = 8596624
- Pad to 8 digits: 08596624
- Extract middle 4 digits: **5966**
- Random number R₃ = 0.5966

### Iteration 4
- Square: (5966)² = 35593156
- Pad to 8 digits: 35593156
- Extract middle 4 digits: **5931**
- Random number R₄ = 0.5931

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Linear Congruential Generator (LCG)

**Problem:** Generate 5 random numbers using LCG with seed x₀ = 7, a = 5, c = 3, m = 16.

Formula: **xₙ₊₁ = (a·xₙ + c) mod m**

### Iteration 1
- x₁ = (5 × 7 + 3) mod 16 = 38 mod 16 = **6**
- R₁ = 6/16 = 0.375

### Iteration 2
- x₂ = (5 × 6 + 3) mod 16 = 33 mod 16 = **1**
- R₂ = 1/16 = 0.0625

### Iteration 3
- x₃ = (5 × 1 + 3) mod 16 = 8 mod 16 = **8**
- R₃ = 8/16 = 0.500

### Iteration 4
- x₄ = (5 × 8 + 3) mod 16 = 43 mod 16 = **11**
- R₄ = 11/16 = 0.6875

### Iteration 5
- x₅ = (5 × 11 + 3) mod 16 = 58 mod 16 = **10**
- R₅ = 10/16 = 0.625

---

## 3.7 Worked Example: Monte Carlo Integration to Estimate π

**Problem:** Use Monte Carlo method with 5 sample points uniformly distributed in [0,1] × [0,1] to estimate π.

Test: A point (x, y) is inside the quarter circle if **x² + y² ≤ 1**.

| Sample | x | y | x² + y² | Inside? |
|--------|---|---|---------|---------|
| 1 | 0.42 | 0.71 | 0.1764 + 0.5041 = 0.6805 | ✓ |
| 2 | 0.13 | 0.55 | 0.0169 + 0.3025 = 0.3194 | ✓ |
| 3 | 0.81 | 0.92 | 0.6561 + 0.8464 = 1.5025 | ✗ |
| 4 | 0.28 | 0.34 | 0.0784 + 0.1156 = 0.1940 | ✓ |
| 5 | 0.66 | 0.45 | 0.4356 + 0.2025 = 0.6381 | ✓ |

Estimate of π = 4 × (Hits / Total) = 4 × (4/5) = **3.20**

True value: 3.14159. With only 5 samples, error is significant — illustrating that **Monte Carlo convergence is slow (O(1/√N))**.

---

## 3.8 Worked Example: Manual Queuing Simulation

**Problem:** Simulate an M/M/1 queue for first 5 customers. Arrivals and service are exponential with means 1/λ = 2 min, 1/μ = 1.8 min. Use R₁...R₁₀ for inter-arrival and service.

**Random number mapping:**
- Inter-arrival A_i = -2 · ln(R_i)
- Service time S_i = -1.8 · ln(R_i)

| Customer | R(A) | Inter-arrival A | Arrival Time | R(S) | Service S | Service Start | Service End | Wait Time |
|----------|------|-----------------|--------------|------|-----------|---------------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | 0.45 | 1.596 | 1.596 | 0.18 | 3.090 | 1.596 | 4.686 | 0 |
| 2 | 0.30 | 2.408 | 4.004 | 0.62 | 0.857 | 4.686 | 5.543 | 0.682 |
| 3 | 0.85 | 0.325 | 4.329 | 0.40 | 2.370 | 5.543 | 7.913 | 1.214 |
| 4 | 0.55 | 1.194 | 5.523 | 0.75 | 0.520 | 7.913 | 8.433 | 2.390 |
| 5 | 0.20 | 3.219 | 8.742 | 0.05 | 5.336 | 8.742 | 14.078 | 0 |

Average wait Wq ≈ (0 + 0.682 + 1.214 + 2.390 + 0)/5 = **0.857 min**

Compare with theoretical: Wq = λ/(μ(μ−λ)). Here λ = 1/2 = 0.5/min, μ = 1/1.8 ≈ 0.556/min → ρ = 0.9 → Wq = 0.5/(0.556·0.056) ≈ 16 min (theory says very high because ρ is near 1).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the M/M/1 queueing model. State any three performance measures used to evaluate it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **M/M/1 queueing model** is a single-server queueing system where:
- Inter-arrival times are **exponentially distributed** (Markovian arrivals — Poisson process) with parameter λ.
- Service times are **exponentially distributed** with parameter μ.
- A **single server** provides service on a **First-Come-First-Served (FCFS)** basis with infinite queue capacity.

Three performance measures (any 3):
1. **Server Utilization:** ρ = λ/μ
2. **Expected number in system:** L = ρ/(1−ρ)
3. **Expected waiting time:** Wq = λ/[μ(μ−λ)]
4. **Probability of zero customers:** P0 = 1 − ρ

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is Monte Carlo Simulation? Mention its two main applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Monte Carlo Simulation** is a stochastic computational technique that uses **repeated random sampling** from known probability distributions to obtain numerical estimates for problems that are mathematically intractable.

Two main applications:
1. **Numerical Integration** — e.g., estimating π, multidimensional integrals.
2. **Stochastic Modelling** — e.g., queueing systems, inventory, financial risk analysis (VaR), reliability engineering.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
A small post office has a single clerk serving customers. Customers arrive at the rate of **20 per hour (Poisson)**, and the clerk serves at the rate of **30 per hour (Exponential)**. Compute:
(i) Server utilization ρ
(ii) Probability that the system is empty (P0)
(iii) Expected number in the queue (Lq)
(iv) Expected waiting time in the system (W)
(v) Probability that a customer has to wait.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** λ = 20/hr, μ = 30/hr.

**(i) Server Utilization:**
$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{20}{30} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667$$

**(ii) Probability of empty system:**
$$P_0 = 1 - \rho = 1 - 0.6667 = 0.3333$$

**(iii) Expected number in queue:**
$$L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho} = \frac{(0.6667)^2}{1 - 0.6667} = \frac{0.4444}{0.3333} = 1.3333 \text{ customers}$$

**(iv) Expected time in system:**
$$W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{30 - 20} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1 \text{ hr} = 6 \text{ minutes}$$

**(v) Probability of waiting:**
$$P(\text{wait}) = \rho = 0.6667 \text{ or } 66.67\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **W** (time in system) with **Wq** (time in queue). Remember: **W = Wq + 1/μ** — total time = wait time + service time. Many students forget the 1/μ service component and lose 2 marks.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
A bank has **2 tellers** (c = 2) serving customers from a single queue. Customers arrive at λ = 15/hr (Poisson), and each teller serves at μ = 12/hr (Exponential). Find:
(i) Traffic intensity ρ
(ii) Probability that an arriving customer has to wait (Pw) using Erlang-C
(iii) Expected number in queue (Lq)
(iv) Expected waiting time in queue (Wq)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** λ = 15/hr, μ = 12/hr, c = 2.

**(i) Traffic intensity:**
$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c\mu} = \frac{15}{2 \times 12} = \frac{15}{24} = 0.625$$

Since ρ < 1, system is stable. (cρ = 2 × 0.625 = 1.25)

**(ii) Erlang-C Probability of Waiting:**

$$P_w = \frac{(c\rho)^c / c!}{(1-\rho)\sum_{k=0}^{c-1}\frac{(c\rho)^k}{k!} + \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!}}$$

Substituting c = 2, cρ = 1.25, ρ = 0.625:

- Sum term: $\sum_{k=0}^{1}\frac{(1.25)^k}{k!} = \frac{(1.25)^0}{0!} + \frac{(1.25)^1}{1!} = 1 + 1.25 = 2.25$
- Numerator: $(1.25)^2 / 2! = 1.5625 / 2 = 0.78125$
- Denominator: $(1 - 0.625)(2.25) + 0.78125 = (0.375)(2.25) + 0.78125 = 0.84375 + 0.78125 = 1.625$

$$P_w = \frac{0.78125}{1.625} = 0.4808$$

So **47.92%** of customers must wait.

**(iii) Expected number in queue:**
$$L_q = P_w \cdot \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} = 0.4808 \times \frac{0.625}{0.375} = 0.4808 \times 1.6667 = 0.8013$$

**(iv) Expected waiting time in queue:**
$$W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda} = \frac{0.8013}{15} = 0.0534 \text{ hr} \approx 3.21 \text{ minutes}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
Explain the **Mid-Square Method** for generating random numbers with a suitable example. What are the limitations of this method?

**Model Solution:**

**Mid-Square Method** (proposed by **John von Neumann** in 1949):
1. Start with an **n-digit seed** x₀.
2. **Square** the seed to get x₀² (which has up to 2n digits).
3. **Pad with leading zeros** to make it exactly 2n digits.
4. **Extract the middle n digits** → next random number x₁.
5. **Repeat** the process using x₁ to get x₂, and so on.
6. Convert to uniform random number: Rᵢ = xᵢ / 10ⁿ.

**Example (with 4-digit seed x₀ = 2187):**

| Step | x | x² | x² (8 digits) | Middle 4 digits | R |
|------|---|----|----------------|-----------------|---|
| 0 | 2187 | 4782969 | 04782969 | 2187 (seed) | — |
| 1 | 2187 | 4782969 | 04782969 | 7829 | 0.7829 |
| 2 | 7829 | 61293241 | 61293241 | 2932 | 0.2932 |
| 3 | 2932 | 8596624 | 08596624 | 5966 | 0.5966 |
| 4 | 5966 | 35593156 | 35593156 | 5931 | 0.5931 |

**Limitations:**
1. **Degenerate to Zero:** If the middle digits become 0000, all subsequent numbers remain zero.
2. **Short Cycle Length:** For small seeds, the sequence often repeats quickly.
3. **Not Uniformly Distributed** for large samples.
4. **Seed-Dependent:** Poor choice of seed can lead to highly non-random output.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
A workshop has a single machine. Inter-arrival times of jobs (in minutes) follow an exponential distribution with mean **4 minutes**, and service times are exponential with mean **3 minutes**. Simulate the system for the first **4 arrivals** using the random numbers R = 0.35, 0.62, 0.18, 0.74, 0.05, 0.83, 0.91, 0.40 for inter-arrival and service times respectively. Compute average waiting time and average queue length.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Mean inter-arrival = 4 min → λ = 1/4, Mean service = 3 min → μ = 1/3.

**Formulas:**
- Inter-arrival: A_i = −4 · ln(R)
- Service time: S_i = −3 · ln(R)

### Simulation Table

| Customer | R(A) | A_i (min) | Arrival Time | R(S) | S_i (min) | Service Begin | Service End | Wait Time | Queue Len |
|----------|------|-----------|--------------|------|-----------|---------------|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | 0.35 | 4.197 | 4.197 | 0.62 | 1.434 | 4.197 | 5.631 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 0.18 | 6.840 | 11.037 | 0.74 | 0.911 | 11.037 | 11.948 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 0.05 | 11.983 | 23.020 | 0.83 | 0.559 | 23.020 | 23.579 | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | 0.91 | 0.378 | 23.398 | 0.40 | 2.749 | 23.579 | 26.328 | 0.181 | 0 |

### Computations in Detail:

**Customer 1:**
- A₁ = −4 · ln(0.35) = −4 · (−1.0498) = 4.197 min
- S₁ = −3 · ln(0.62) = −3 · (−0.4780) = 1.434 min
- Server idle at arrival (t = 4.197). Wait = 0. End = 4.197 + 1.434 = 5.631.

**Customer 2:**
- A₂ = −4 · ln(0.18) = −4 · (−1.7148) = 6.840 min
- Arrival = 4.197 + 6.840 = 11.037 min. Server idle since 5.631. Wait = 0.

**Customer 3:**
- A₃ = −4 · ln(0.05) = −4 · (−2.9957) = 11.983 min
- Arrival = 11.037 + 11.983 = 23.020 min. Server idle. Wait = 0.

**Customer 4:**
- A₄ = −4 · ln(0.91) = −4 · (−0.0943) = 0.378 min
- Arrival = 23.020 + 0.378 = 23.398 min. Server busy until 23.579. Wait = 23.579 − 23.398 = 0.181 min.

### Results

- **Average Waiting Time:** Wq = (0 + 0 + 0 + 0.181) / 4 = **0.045 min**
- **Average Queue Length:** Lq = λ · Wq = (1/4) · 0.045 = **0.0113 customers**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Three Common Errors:**
> 1. Forgetting the **negative sign** in the exponential transformation: A = +4·ln(R) is WRONG; it gives positive inputs only for R > 1, which is impossible. A = **−**4·ln(R) is correct.
> 2. Confusing **service begin time** with **arrival time** when the server is busy. Begin = max(arrival, previous service end).
> 3. Computing **Lq as λ·Wq** is valid here only in steady-state; for short simulations, compute it as (Σ waiting customers × time in queue) / total time. Using Little's law is the simpler KTU-accepted method.

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Revision Checklist — Module 4 High-Yield Points**

### **M/M/1 Single-Server Queue**
- ⭐ Stability requires **ρ = λ/μ < 1**.
- ⭐ **P0 = 1 − ρ**; **Pn = (1−ρ)·ρⁿ** (geometric distribution).
- ⭐ **L = ρ/(1−ρ) = λ/(μ−λ)**.
- ⭐ **Lq = ρ²/(1−ρ)**.
- ⭐ **W = 1/(μ−λ)**; **Wq = λ/[μ(μ−λ)]**.
- ⭐ **Probability of waiting = ρ** for M/M/1 (very common KTU trick question!).
- ⭐ **Little's Law always holds:** L = λW, Lq = λWq.

### **M/M/c Multi-Server Queue**
- ⭐ **Traffic intensity:** ρ = λ/(cμ); must have ρ < 1.
- ⭐ **Erlang-C formula** gives probability of waiting — memorize it.
- ⭐ **Lq = Pw · ρ/(1−ρ)** (multi-server specific formula).
- ⭐ **Wq = Lq/λ**; **W = Wq + 1/μ**; **L = Lq + λ/μ**.

### **Monte Carlo Simulation**
- ⭐ A **5-step methodology**: Define → Model → Sample → Compute → Aggregate.
- ⭐ **Convergence rate O(1/√N)** — slower than deterministic methods.
- ⭐ Used for integration, optimization, and stochastic modeling.

### **Queuing Simulation**
- ⭐ **Inter-arrival:** A_i = −(1/λ)·ln(R).
- ⭐ **Service time:** S_i = −(1/μ)·ln(R).
- ⭐ **Event-based clock advance:** Next event = min(next arrival, next departure).

### **Random Number Generation**
- ⭐ **Mid-Square Method:** Square and extract middle digits — simple but degenerates.
- ⭐ **Multiplicative LCG:** x_{n+1} = (a·x_n) mod m.
- ⭐ **Mixed LCG:** x_{n+1} = (a·x_n + c) mod m.
- ⭐ **Tests for randomness:** Chi-Square (uniformity), Runs Test (independence), Kolmogorov-Smirnov (distribution fit), Autocorrelation (serial independence).
- ⭐ **Properties:** Uniformity, Independence, Efficiency, Reproducibility, Long Period.
